EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Prolonged snowfall produced heavy accumulations in extreme northeast Nebraska, ranging up to 20 inches near the Missouri River. The snowfall took place from two days before to the day after Christmas. The snowfall was accompanied by increasing north to northwest winds which caused widespread blizzard conditions on Christmas day and the start of the next day. The blizzard, added to the very heavy snowfall, made almost all travel impossible, and shut down commerce.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow combined with increasing winds to produce widespread blizzard conditions. The snowfall over 3 days varied from 12 to 20 inches, with the heaviest accumulations near the Missouri River. North to northwest winds reached sustained speeds over 30 mph and there were gusts over 40 mph. These winds combined with the heavy snowfall to produce widespread visibilities below a quarter mile from Christmas day to the start of the next day. Most roads were blocked and travel was brought to a standstill, as was the limited holiday commerce.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 6 to 10 inches over extreme northeast Nebraska was accompanied by north to northwest winds sustained at 30 to 40 mph, with some gusts over 50 mph. The combination of snow and strong winds created widespread blizzard conditions with visibilities near zero. The blizzard closed schools and businesses and halted travel. The snowfall began on the morning of December 8th. The winds increased during the evening of the 8th, and became strong enough to cause the blizzard after midnight on December 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: New snowfall of 7 to 10 inches was accompanied by north to northwest winds sustained at 30 to 40 mph, with gusts up to 50 mph. These strong winds produced widespread blizzard conditions beginning a little after midnight on December 9th. Schools and many businesses were closed on December 9th, as the snowfall and near zero visibilities made travel impossible that morning. Blizzard conditions abated that afternoon as winds steadily decreased, although hazardous wind chills which began during the blizzard continued that night and into the early morning of December 10th. Actual new snowfall reports included 9 inches several miles southwest of Hubbard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense upper level low pressure area dropped southeast across the High Plains during the day, tightening the surface pressure gradient as it passed.  Northwest winds increased to 45-60 mph by mid-morning over much of northwest Kansas and southwest Nebraska.

A low pressure area moved from the Central Rockies across the Central High Plains, producing an area of heavy snow over portions of northwest Kansas and southwest Nebraska.  Snowfall up to 11 inches was reported.  Gusty north winds resulted in some blowing and drifting of snow.  Maximum snow totals in Nebraska included: Six inches in Hitchcock and Red Willow counties.  In Kansas, six inches fell in Rawlins, Sherman and Wallace counties, seven inches in Thomas and Logan counties, eight inches in Sheridan county, ten inches in Decatur, Norton and Gove counties and eleven inches in Graham county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five to six inches of snow was reported in Hitchcock County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense low pressure system over Iowa resulted in a strong surface pressure gradient across the Tri-State region.  Northwest winds of 40 to 60 mph persisted for much of Christmas Day, creating areas of blowing snow and reduced visibility.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak wind gust of 61 mph occurred at 0008 CST on December 26th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air plunged southeast across eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa on January 14th and 15th.  Temperatures fell into the single digits above and below zero during the morning hours of 1/14, recovered a little in the afternoon and then fell to 5 below to 20 below, or a bit colder, by the morning of the 15th.  Northwest winds of 20 to 30 mph with higher gusts were observed into the afternoon of the 14th before subsiding in the afternoon and evening.  This produced wind chill values of 20 below to 30 below zero over much of the region.  By around midnight on the 15th most reporting sites observed winds dropped below 10 mph and remained that way through the morning of the 15th.  However, several locations, mainly south through southeast of Omaha, recorded wind speeds of 10 to 15 mph and wind chill values colder than 20 below through around 5am CST on the 15th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As temperatures fell below zero across the county, northwest winds of 20 to 30 mph in the morning and early afternoon and 10 to 20 mph from late afternoon into late evening, produced wind chill values of 20 below to 30 below zero.  Winds dropped mostly below 10 mph by the early morning hours of 1/15 but air temperatures fell to 10 below to 20 below zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moist air overran a cold surface airmass and produced snow across much of the Nebraska panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Total snowfall during the period was estimated to be around 9 inches in Scottsbluff, NE.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air that spilled in behind an early December Winter Storm/Blizzard that hit eastern Nebraska combined with northwest winds of 15 to 25 mph and produced dangerously cold wind chill values.  Wind chill values of 20 below to 25 below zero were observed during the morning hours of December 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill Values of 20 below to 25 below zero were observed much of the morning hours of December 9th.  Temperatures warmed a little in the afternoon as winds subsided, this allowed wind chill values to moderate by afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic cold front that moved across the Omaha metro area during the early afternoon of January 3 caused a light drizzle that was falling across the area to freeze quickly.  Temperatures which were a few degrees above freezing before the front passed dropped into the 20s within an hour.  At Omaha-Eppley the temperature dropped from 34 at 104 pm to 28 at 123 pm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures across the Omaha metro area were 34 or 35 degrees during the morning of January 3rd as a light drizzle developed.  An Arctic cold front that moved across the area during the early afternoon caused temperatures to fall into the upper 20s within a half hours time causing the drizzle to quickly freeze.  Many streets, parking lots and sidewalks became very slick and hazardous due to the sudden nature of the freeze even though precipitation amounts were only a trace or perhaps a hundredth of an inch or so.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong north to northwest winds, gusting to over 50 mph, combined with existing loose snow cover and 1 to 2 inches of new snow to produce blizzard conditions with widespread visibilities near zero over extreme northeast Nebraska during the day and early evening of January 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: North to northwest winds sustained at 30 to 40 mph with gusts around 50 mph combined with several inches of loose snow cover, along with 1 to 3 inches of new snow, to produce widespread visibilities of a quarter mile or less in blowing snow. The low visibilities and drifting snow made travel impossible in many areas, and forced schools and businesses to close.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked from the Oklahoma panhandle into the Ohio Valley from February 13th through the 14th.  This system brought heavy snow to around a 50 mile wide swath across eastern Nebraska and western Iowa generally along and north of Interstate 80 in eastern Nebraska and along and south of the interstate in western Iowa.  The heaviest snow stretched from Columbus and David City Nebraska east through Omaha and then to Underwood and Oakland Iowa.  On the southern end the snowfall tapered down to several inches from southeast Nebraska into far southwest Iowa.  Meanwhile, the snow ended rather abruptly on the northern end with little or no snow measured along and north of a line from Norfolk Nebraska to Little Sioux Iowa.  Heavier snow totals in Nebraska included 7.5 inches in David City and 6 to 8 inches across much of the Omaha metro area including 6.8 inches at the NWS office near Valley and 5.5 inches at Omaha Eppley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell over all of Sarpy county with 7 inches reported by cooperative observers in Gretna and Bellevue and 6.5 inches by the coop observer in Papillion.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low-level jet ahead of an upper level disturbance brought elevated instability to eastern Nebraska during the morning hours of February 26th.  Despite surface temperatures in the 30s, the elevated instability helped spawn a few severe thunderstorms over eastern Nebraska and western Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to penny size was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Some snow-melt combined with a little rain and warming temperatures helped create an ice jam on the Elkhorn River which caused flooding around and downstream of West Point.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ice jam downstream of West Point caused flooding along the Elkhorn River near and downstream of West Point.  The flooding caused the closure of Highway 275 from the Jct of Highway 91 to the Dodge county line. The flooding also crossed a county road near Scribner and flooded a few farm fields around Crowell.  Damage was minimal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A compact upper level system that tracked from the northern Rockies across central Nebraska and into eastern Kansas brought a 12 to 18 hour period of snow to eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa.  Although the snow was briefly heavy, water equivalent was generally fairly low and most accumulations over the region were less than 6 inches.  However, isolated locations received 6 or more inches, especially across Pierce county where 6 to 9 inches fell over much of the county.  Even though north winds occasionally gusted over 20 mph, blowing and drifting snow was not a major issue with this storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 5 to 8 inches was reported over much of Cedar county.  Randolph picked up 8 inches for the highest amount reported in the county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell over all of Douglas county with 6.8 inches measured at the NWS office near Valley and 5.5 inches at Omaha Eppley.  Other reports of 6 to 8 inches were received from various sources throughout the county.

Meanwhile, the snow ended rather abruptly on the northern end with little or no snow measured along and north of a line from Norfolk Nebraska to Little Sioux Iowa.  Heavier snow totals in Nebraska included 7.5 inches in David City and 6 to 8 inches across much of the Omaha metro area including 6.8 inches at the NWS office near Valley and 5.5 inches at Omaha Eppley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow was reported across much of Butler county with 7.5 inches reported by the cooperative observer in David City.

Snowfall accompanying the storm in Nebraska included:  Dundy county...2-4.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A supercell thunderstorm produced a tornado and hail during the early evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trained storm spotters reported the touchdown of the tornado approximately 17 miles south southwest of Mullen.  The tornado traveled across open rangeland until encountering one ranch.  As it moved past the ranch, it demolished a metal windbreak, a tower, an outbuilding, and a windmill. The tornado also broke off large trees and carried a pickup cab 100 feet.  The tornado then continued on a east-southeast track carrying debris 2 miles.  The tornado was on the ground for about 3.5 miles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air that spilled in behind an early December Winter Storm/Blizzard that hit eastern Nebraska combined with northwest winds of 15 to 25 mph and produced dangerously cold wind chill values.  Wind chill values of 20 below to 25 below zero were observed during the morning hours of December 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill Values of 20 below to 25 below zero were observed much of the morning hours of December 9th.  Temperatures warmed a little in the afternoon as winds subsided, this allowed wind chill values to moderate by afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air plunged southeast across eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa on January 14th and 15th.  Temperatures fell into the single digits above and below zero during the morning hours of 1/14, recovered a little in the afternoon and then fell to 5 below to 20 below, or a bit colder, by the morning of the 15th.  Northwest winds of 20 to 30 mph with higher gusts were observed into the afternoon of the 14th before subsiding in the afternoon and evening.  This produced wind chill values of 20 below to 30 below zero over much of the region.  By around midnight on the 15th most reporting sites observed winds dropped below 10 mph and remained that way through the morning of the 15th.  However, several locations, mainly south through southeast of Omaha, recorded wind speeds of 10 to 15 mph and wind chill values colder than 20 below through around 5am CST on the 15th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As temperatures fell below zero across the county, northwest winds of 20 to 30 mph in the morning and early afternoon and 10 to 20 mph from late afternoon into late evening, produced wind chill values of 25 below to 30 below zero.  Winds dropped mostly below 10 mph by the early morning hours of 1/15 but air temperatures fell to 10 below to 20 below zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level shortwave trough moving across the region helped to spark off a few late afternoon and evening thunderstorms across portions of south central Nebraska.  A few of these thunderstorms were able to drop pea to quarter size hail, along with some locally heavy rain.

An upper level shortwave trough moving across the region helped to spark off a few late afternoon and evening thunderstorms across portions of south central Nebraska.  A few of these thunderstorms were able to drop pea to quarter size hail, along with some locally heavy rain.

A 40 to 50 knot low level jet nosing into the region brought another round of thunderstorms to portions of south central Nebraska during the morning hours.  With modest instability available, some thunderstorms were strong to severe, dropping penny to ping pong ball size hail.

A strong and slow moving spring storm caused severe thunderstorms to occur across north central Nebraska. As colder was drawn into the system, strong winds and snow combined to produce blizzard conditions across the western half of north central Nebraska.  Snowfall amounts ranged from 3 to 8 inches with snow drifts ranging up to 5 feet across Sheridan county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The strong thunderstorm winds broke off large tree limbs, tore off the roof of a hay shed, overturned a center pivot irrigation system, damaged a grain bin, and overturned a corn crib.

Snowfall amounts ranged from 3 to 8 inches with snow drifts ranging up to 5 feet across Sheridan county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow and strong northwest winds of 30 to 40 mph with gusts to around 60 mph occurred during the afternoon and overnight hours.  Widespread visibility was a quarter mile or less.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down briefly on the north side of the Bob Kruger ranch.  The tornado lifted an old trailer home south southwestward about 20 yards and destroyed it.  It also moved a hay baler 10 yards and turned it around.  It snapped off numerous tree limbs, tore metal siding off a quonset, and destroyed a metal wind break.  Numerous storm chasers captured the intense supercell on video.  Chasers reported inflow winds into the supercell of up to 70 mph.

Supercell storms developed across western and north central Nebraska during the afternoon of June 17th and produced very large hail, heavy rain, funnels, and a brief tornado.  As the evening progressed, the storms transitioned into bow echoes and produced strong straight-line winds during the early morning hours of June 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail was accompanied by strong winds and stripped leaves off the trees.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado developed on the northwest corner of a bow echo and touched down very briefly over Union Pacific Railroad Bailey Yard and was witnessed by numerous railroad workers.  The tornado derailed 14 empty coal cars and overturned a semi in Bailey Yard. It also snapped off large trees on the north edge of Bailey Yard before lifting.  The storm had produced another brief tornado previous to this one.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Management reported numerous roads washed out from heavy rain.  Road lengths of up to 150 feet were washed out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A supercell dropped south out of South Dakota and into north central Nebraska late in the evening.  The supercell produced strong winds...large hail...and 2 brief tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rock County spotters reported a funnel cloud just north and west of Newport.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rock County sheriff reported a funnel cloud along Highway 137 north of Newport.

Thunderstorms moved southeast during the early morning hours, producing a few reports of damaging thunderstorm winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observer reported strong thunderstorm winds broke a large 8-9 inch diameter tree branch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed and intensified over eastern Colorado during the early afternoon hours and moved across southwest Nebraska and northwest Kansas by late afternoon. Numerous reports of strong thunderstorm winds were received which produced minor damage. A few marginally severe hail reports were also received.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Outflow wind gust was well away from the originating thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe storm produced some hail and strong winds over parts of Dawes county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail was reported from 1/2 up to 1 inch in diameter over the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upslope flow created scattered thunderstorms with one severe storm having produced some hail and minor flooding over southern Cheyenne county.
Upslope flow created scattered thunderstorms with one severe storm having produced some hail and minor flooding over southern Cheyenne county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail was estimated to range in size from 3/4 up to 1.5 inches in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upslope flow created scattered thunderstorms with one severe storm having produced some hail and minor flooding over southern Cheyenne county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall caused some flooding of county roads 8 and 121.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed near a cold front with one storm producing some large hail.
A line of thunderstorms produced gusty winds and some hail over mainly the siuthern Nebraska panhandle.

A slow moving cold front kicked off a few severe thunderstorms over eastern Nebraska during the mid evening hours of July 24th.  Initially warm mid level temperatures had hindered development, but they were overcome as the front encountered a bit better low level inflow and instability.
A thunderstorm produced damaging winds which caused tree damage from Newcastle to Martinsburg in extreme northeast Nebraska on the evening of July 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused an unknown amount of tree damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed and intensified over eastern Colorado during the early afternoon hours and moved across southwest Nebraska and northwest Kansas by late afternoon. Numerous reports of strong thunderstorm winds were received which produced minor damage. A few marginally severe hail reports were also received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The caller also reported wind gusts estimated at 50 mph and one inch of rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed and intensified over eastern Colorado during the early afternoon hours and moved across southwest Nebraska and northwest Kansas by late afternoon. Numerous reports of strong thunderstorm winds were received which produced minor damage. A few marginally severe hail reports were also received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning started a pasture fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm complex that developed over South Dakota during the evening of July 7th moved southeast along an instability gradient and down the Missouri River reaching the Omaha area before weakening.  The storms mainly produced strong winds but some hail was also reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts were measured at 63 mph at the Asos at Omaha-Eppley.  The winds caused scattered tree damage in east and south Omaha which also caused some power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm complex that developed over South Dakota during the evening of July 7th moved southeast along an instability gradient and down the Missouri River reaching the Omaha area before weakening.  The storms mainly produced strong winds but some hail was also reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds were estimated at 60 mph by emergency management from around 5 miles to 7 miles east of Pender.  The winds were accompanied by penny-size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm complex that developed over South Dakota during the evening of July 7th moved southeast along an instability gradient and down the Missouri River reaching the Omaha area before weakening.  The storms mainly produced strong winds but some hail was also reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail was accompanied by wind gusts estimated at 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm complex that developed over South Dakota during the evening of July 7th moved southeast along an instability gradient and down the Missouri River reaching the Omaha area before weakening.  The storms mainly produced strong winds but some hail was also reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated at close to 60 mph knocked down tree limbs in the Bellevue area causing some power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed along a warm front that moved into central Nebraska during the afternoon of July 22.  The storms produced damaging winds and large hail, although most of the damage from them remained just west of the region over central Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter estimated hail reached penny to quarter-size a little west of Columbus.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed along a warm front that moved into central Nebraska during the afternoon of July 22.  The storms produced damaging winds and large hail, although most of the damage from them remained just west of the region over central Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter estimated that thunderstorm winds reached 60 mph in the Milford area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front kicked off a few severe thunderstorms over eastern Nebraska during the mid evening hours of July 24th.  Initially warm mid level temperatures had hindered development, but they were overcome as the front encountered a bit better low level inflow and instability.

Isolated strong thunderstorms tracked across far northeast and far southeast Nebraska during the predawn hours of July 10. The storms formed near weak boundaries that were in place across the area.  Warm mid level temperatures apparently helped limit the areal coverage of the storms in the morning and suppressed additional activity later that afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail may have reached quarter-size according to a public report.  The hail was accompanied by winds of at least 40 mph which caused some crop damage in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance combined with the return flow of warmer and more unstable air and fueled a thunderstorm complex that dropped down the Missouri River Vally during the mid to late morning hours of July 12th.  The storms produced mainly strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public estimated that Thunderstorm wind gusts reached 55 to 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front kicked off a few severe thunderstorms over eastern Nebraska during the mid evening hours of July 24th.  Initially warm mid level temperatures had hindered development, but they were overcome as the front encountered a bit better low level inflow and instability.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds were estimated at 60 to 70 mph.  The winds downed some trees and knocked power to sections of town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upslope surface flow created a line of thunderstorms over mainly the northern Nebraska panhandle which produced some strong winds and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brief wind gusts from storm outflow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upslope surface flow created a line of thunderstorms over mainly the northern Nebraska panhandle which produced some strong winds and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong outflow winds from line of thunderstorms as it moved across Dawes county.  Report of a tree blown onto a car near Fort Robinson State Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upslope surface flow created a line of thunderstorms over mainly the northern Nebraska panhandle which produced some strong winds and hail.
Thunderstorms produced large hail, damaging winds, and one case of flash flooding in Dixon and Dakota Counties of extreme northeast Nebraska on the morning of July 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large hail stripped leaves from trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced large hail, damaging winds, and one case of flash flooding in Dixon and Dakota Counties of extreme northeast Nebraska on the morning of July 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds destroyed a farm building.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced large hail, damaging winds, and one case of flash flooding in Dixon and Dakota Counties of extreme northeast Nebraska on the morning of July 9th.
Thunderstorms produced large hail, damaging winds, and one case of flash flooding in Dixon and Dakota Counties of extreme northeast Nebraska on the morning of July 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large hail was accompanied by damaging winds and very heavy rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced large hail, damaging winds, and one case of flash flooding in Dixon and Dakota Counties of extreme northeast Nebraska on the morning of July 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rain caused flash flooding of streets in the town of Newcastle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced large hail, damaging winds, and one case of flash flooding in Dixon and Dakota Counties of extreme northeast Nebraska on the morning of July 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large hail broke windows on vehicles, including that of the spotter who reported the hail. The hail also dented vehicles. The amount of damage was not known.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced large hail, damaging winds, and one case of flash flooding in Dixon and Dakota Counties of extreme northeast Nebraska on the morning of July 9th.

A strong upper level low pressure system moving out of the desert southwest and crossing the central and southern plains brought snow starting during the early morning hours of the 13th.  As this system shifted through Kansas, this placed portions of south central Nebraska in an area of enhanced lift, allowing for the accumulation of several inches of snow.  Winds generally remained between 10 and 20 mph, with gusts near 25 to 30 mph at times, and combined with the at times heavy snow, caused periodic reductions in visibility down to a half to a quarter of a mile.  Locations generally along and north of Interstate 80 saw the highest snow accumulations, with numerous reports of 6 to 9 inches.  South of Interstate 80, accumulations were slightly lower, ranging from 4 to 7 inches.  This storm caused the closing of many schools and the postponement of events across the area.

An area of low pressure moved out of the Rockies and onto the plains. A strong pressure gradient created high winds for several hours. Most of the area observed winds gusting over 40 mph with some localized sites seeing winds in excess of 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at the Alliance airport measured a wind gust of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved out of the Rockies and onto the plains. A strong pressure gradient created high winds for several hours. Most of the area observed winds gusting over 40 mph with some localized sites seeing winds in excess of 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A report of an estimated wind gust of 58 mph was received from the Scotts Bluff National Monument.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across most of the Tri-State region during the late afternoon and evening hours, producing hail up to golf ball size, wind gusts to 66 mph and several tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: No known damage resulted from this tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Some weak showers produced a few dry microbursts over parts of the Nebraska panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dry microburst at the Scotts Bluff county airport.  A few power lines were knocked down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced some strong wind gusts over parts of Cheyenne county.

Winds generally remained between 10 and 20 mph, with gusts near 25 to 30 mph at times, and combined with the at times heavy snow, caused periodic reductions in visibility down to a half to a quarter of a mile.  Locations generally along and north of Interstate 80 saw the highest snow accumulations, with numerous reports of 6 to 9 inches.  South of Interstate 80, accumulations were slightly lower, ranging from 4 to 7 inches.  This storm caused the closing of many schools and the postponement of events across the area.
A strong low pressure system over eastern Colorado produced blizzard conditions over parts of Kimball county.  Interstate 80 was closed for a time due to many accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds combined with 2 to 4 inches of snow to produce blowing snow and very low visibilities for a time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moderately strong low-level jet ahead of a cold front helped to kick off a narrow line of thunderstorms that tracked repeatedly across northeast Nebraska.  The storms initially produced isolated severe weather but turn into mainly heavy rain producers.  Two to as much as four inches fell in a small area from southwest of Neligh Nebraska to near Sioux City Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Some hailstones reached around nickel-size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early morning high-based thunderstorms moved across southeast Nebraska.  One storm produced a lightning strike which caused damage to electrical equipment at the Johnson county Sheriff's office.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike caused around $25,000 worth of damage to computers, radio and dispatch equipment and the phone system at the Sheriff's office in Tecumseh.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front which moved into southeast Nebraska during the morning of June 6th stalled from southeast Nebraska into central Kansas during the afternoon and evening of that day.  Temperatures by late afternoon climbed into the upper 80s and lower 90s south of the front while readings northwest of the front were in the 50s and 60s. Thunderstorms developed near the boundary over Kansas early in the evening, and developed or spread into southeast Nebraska during the mid evening hours.  The storms produced mainly large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported from an off-duty NWS employee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced damaging winds at Newcastle in Dixon County on the early morning of June 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused tree damage, including a few trees blown down. The winds also blew down power lines, causing a power outage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled front extending from extreme southeast Nebraska into central Kansas, and an upper level low pressure trough to the west, brought scattered severe thunderstorms to eastern Nebraska during much of Sunday June 7th into the very early morning hours of June 8th.  Temperatures climbed into the lower 80s in far southeast Nebraska and southwest Iowa on the 7th, while readings remained in the 50s in northeast Nebraska.  Elevated storms, a few of which dropped large hail, moved across northeast Nebraska from late morning through the afternoon hours of the 7th.  By late afternoon of that day several supercell thunderstorms developed along the stalled front.  These storms shifted out of the region by early evening but additional thunderstorms that developed near the boundary in Kansas moved back into southeast Nebraska later that evening into the early overnight hours.  Although the environment was favorable for tornadoes this evening, in Nebraska the storms produced large hail and a few strong wind reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail dented at least one car in the area.  The hail could have been larger according to the person who reported it the next day.

The south end of a small cluster of thunderstorms that moved out of central Nebraska turned severe a little after sunrise in southeast Nebraska.  An isolated storm produced large hail and gusty winds as it tracked from York county Nebraska east into Cass county.  The storm turned severe as it encountered unstable conditions in southeast Nebraska which was located on the northeast nose of warm mid level temperatures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to golf ball-size was reported near Pleasant Dale.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front, possibly reinforced by thunderstorms that occurred either earlier in the afternoon or during the previous evening, caused a few severe thunderstorms to develop from southwest Iowa into southeast Nebraska.  Due to hot and humid conditions present over the region, the thunderstorms were mainly the wet micro-burst variety although some hail was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated at around 60 mph were also reported in the area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter estimated thunderstorm wind gusts around 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed southeast out of South Dakota into northeast Nebraska during the early morning hours of June 25th.  At least one hail report was received from a storm as it tracked across northeast Nebraska, but the activity quickly weakened after that.

Thunderstorms developed along and north of a warm front that extended across northern Kansas during the mid evening hours of June 15th.  The storms produced very heavy rain, and some locations picked up 2 inches or so in an hour or two, which produced localized flash flooding.  An isolated storm also produce large hail in Seward county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel-size hail was reported along Interstate 80.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed over eastern Nebraska during the morning hours of August 13th.  The thunderstorms developed as a modest low level jet ushered in warm and moist advection across eastern Nebraska.  An isolated storm produced hail just under 1 inch in diameter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel-size hail covered the ground white near Wisner stripping some crops in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms developed across the central Sandhills of Nebraska producing heavy rain and hail.

Moist and unstable conditions that prevailed over eastern Nebraska during the afternoon and evening hours of August 15th helped set the stage for slow moving thunderstorms that repeatedly developed and tracked across parts of northeast Nebraska.  The storms were aided by an unseasonably strong upper level system that was tracking along the Montana/Canadian border.  The storms developed in a broken west to east line generally to the northwest through north of Columbus.  The storms were eventually overtaken around midnight by a larger and generally north to south line of thunderstorms that had developed earlier that day over the western high plains.  The storms in northeast Nebraska were mainly heavy rain producers, but a few possessed strong rotation and brought some hail and gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain producing thunderstorms caused flash flooding across some county roads from around Pierce to near Plainview.  Besides washing the gravel off of a few county roads, some crops in low areas also sustained damage.   Pierce had 4.13 inches from the storm with 4.18 inches reported several miles south of Plainview.  The rain began falling during the late afternoon hours of the 15th and persisted a bit past midnight on the 16th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense low pressure system in the Southern Plains lifted northward into Missouri on Thursday, December 24th, and into western Iowa on Friday, December 25th, 2009.  This resulted in moderate to heavy snowfall and blizzard conditions across much of central and north central Nebraska with strong north winds of 30 to 40 mph with gusts to 50 mph.  Most county highways and rural roads became closed during the morning of Friday, December 25th, as snowfall amounts ranged from 4 to 15 inches.  Many snow drifts ranged from 4 to 8 feet, with a few as high as 10 feet.  Persistent snowfall across the Pine Ridge in the northern Panhandle allowed snowfall amounts to range up to 36 inches in northern Sheridan County with snowdrifts from 3 to 10 feet and road closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from 7 to 12 inches across Boyd County.  Strong north winds of 30 to 40 mph with gusts to 50 mph created blizzard conditions and visibilities below a quarter mile through the afternoon and into the evening hours on December 25, 2009.  Snow drifts from 4 to 8 feet were common and virtually all roads became closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large and relatively slow moving storm brought a prolonged winter storm and even, for a time, blizzard conditions to eastern Nebraska and western Iowa from late on Dec. 7th through the early morning hours of Dec. 9th.  The storm was driven by an upper level disturbance that tracked from the west coast across the Rockies and deepened over the Mississippi River Valley.  The heaviest snow fell in advance of when the stronger winds arrived, mainly during the morning and afternoon of Dec. 8th.  However, as north winds increased to 30 to 50 mph during the night of the 8th and early on the 9th, visibilities frequently dropped to near zero, especially in open areas.  Considerable drifting snow also occurred, in many cases closing roads back shut almost as fast as they could be opened.  This prompted many counties to pull snow plows off the roads for a while during the night of the 8th and early on the 9th.  Many schools were closed for 3 days because of the storm; due the forecast of heavy snow to begin on the 7th, because of poor visibilities and roads that were drifted shut on the 8th and still some closed roads and bitter cold wind chills which followed the storm on the 9th.  An elderly Omaha man was found dead during the evening of the 8th when he apparently had car trouble and returned to his apartment and was found dead sitting down in a chair outside. ||Total snowfall from the storm was 6 to 15 inches over most of eastern Nebraska and western Iowa.  Higher amounts in eastern Nebraska included 15 inches in Union, 14 inches in Columbus, around 12 inches at the NWS in Valley, at Tekamah, Uehling, Steele City and Weeping Water.  In western Iowa heavier amounts included 12 inches in Clarinda and Hastings and around 11 inches in Harlan, Red Oak and Little Sioux.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 8 to 12 inches fell across much of the county from this storm with a little over 12 inches measured at the NWS office in Valley, 9.4 inches measured at Omaha Eppley and around 10.5 inches measured in Bennington and at Boystown. The snow began during the late evening of 12/7 and continued into the early morning hours of 12/9.  Winds were not that strong when the snow started, but north winds increased during the afternoon of 12/8 and especially that evening into the next morning.  Frequent wind gusts of 40 mph or more were observed with a peak gust of 52 mph measured by the ASOS at Omaha Eppley. The winds produced considerable blowing and drifting snow with blizzard conditions during the evening and early morning hours of the 8th and 9th. Some county roads were drifted shut and interstate on-ramps and residential roads were difficult to keep open.||An elderly man was found dead outside an Omaha apartment complex late on the 8th after he had sought help for car trouble and then evidently sat down in a chair outdoors, according to police.  A 28-year-old woman was killed late on the 8th when she was hit by a snowplow that was backing up outside of her southwest Omaha apartment complex and a 63-year-old man died of a heart attack on the 9th after shoveling snow outside of his northwest Omaha home.  The weight of the snow damaged several roofs of businesses in Omaha and also caused the city to spend over $500,000 in equipment rental for snow removal besides over $1 million in actual snow removal expenses.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level low pressure system brought a heavy wet snow to far southeast Nebraska.  The precipitation started as rain...mixed with then changed to snow before sunrise on the 16th and then mixed back with rain or changed over to rain later that afternoon.  However, before it did visibilities dropped to a 1/4 mile or less and snowfall rates of around 2 inches an hour were briefly reached.  Total snowfall of 6 to 10 inches was common along and to about 25 miles north of the Kansas border.  Higher amounts included 10 inches in Endicott and 8 inches at Diller and 5 miles northeast of Falls City.  The heavy wet snow caused numerous accidents and the weight of the snow downed tree branches onto power lines causing power losses to around 6,200 customers.  Schools were canceled or let out early on the 16th and several were still closed on the 17th.  Nebraska Emergency Management estimated that the storm caused more than $3.5 million in damages and a federal disaster declaration was approved for the counties involved in the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain changed to a wet heavy snow during the predawn hours of November 16th.  Snowfall rates through mid morning reached an inch or so per hour as visibilities dropped to a quarter mile or less.  Total snowfall was around 3 to 6 inches across most of the county, with the heaviest along the southern border.  The heavy wet snow caused a few accidents and power losses in the morning as the weight of the snow downed large tree limbs which fell on power lines causing them to fall.  However, by afternoon the snow had mostly melted on roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance on the west coast moved southward over the southern Rockies and then out onto the southern plains from approximately December 21st through the 26th. This system affected the Cheyenne CWA in two waves. The first occurred December 22 and 23rd and brought light snow with strong winds that created drifting snow and hazardous driving conditions. As the storm system moved eastward, it intensified and brought heavy snow and blizzard conditions to the central Plains over the Holiday weekend.  A second wave of significant winter weather occurred over southeast Wyoming and the western Nebraska panhandle in wraparound energy from late on the 24th through the 26th.  Heavy snow and very strong winds produced blizzard conditions in the northeast portions of the CWA with near blizzard conditions elsewhere. Many roads were closed, including interstates 80 and 25 on Christmas day. Two deaths were indirectly attributed to to the second wave of winter weather, one near Laramie, WY and the other near Guernsey, WY.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Near blizzard conditions occurred over the southern half of the Nebraska panhandle through Christmas day and continued through the late afternoon of the 26th. Area roads were closed due to snow and blowing snow reducing visibilities and creating hazardous driving conditions.  The AWOS at Kimball measured a wind gust of 58 MPH at 25/1210 MST. Combined with recent snowfall, near blizzard conditions were observed at this time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense low pressure system in the Southern Plains lifted northward into Missouri on Thursday, December 24th, and into western Iowa on Friday, December 25th, 2009.  This resulted in moderate to heavy snowfall and blizzard conditions across much of central and north central Nebraska with strong north winds of 30 to 40 mph with gusts to 50 mph.  Most county highways and rural roads became closed during the morning of Friday, December 25th, as snowfall amounts ranged from 4 to 15 inches.  Many snow drifts ranged from 4 to 8 feet, with a few as high as 10 feet.  Persistent snowfall across the Pine Ridge in the northern Panhandle allowed snowfall amounts to range up to 36 inches in northern Sheridan County with snowdrifts from 3 to 10 feet and road closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest snowfall amounts ranged from 10 to 36 inches across northern Sheridan County.  Strong north winds of 25 to 35 mph created considerable blowing and drifting and visibilities below a half mile at times through the afternoon hours on December 25, 2009.  Snow drifts from 3 to 10 feet were reported across the northern part of the county with road closures.  Snowfall amounts ranged from 6 to 10 inches across the central portions of the county and 2 to 6 inches across the south.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved across the Central Plains on December 8th and 9th, 2009. Moderate to heavy snow accompanied this weather system from southwest Nebraska into eastern portions of north central Nebraska.  Snow began to fall Monday evening, December 7th, and continued into the early morning hours of Wednesday, December 9th.  Storm total snowfall amounts ranged from 5 to 10 inches with the heaviest amounts across Custer County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall reports from Cooperative observers and the public ranged from 5 to 7 inches.  North winds of 15 to 30 mph caused considerable blowing and drifting with reduced visibility as low as a quarter mile at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warmer and more moist air spread north across eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa in advance of a developing complex winter storm that was lifting out of the southern plains.  This produced an ice storm that preceded a Winter Storm and eventual Blizzard that developed across the region.  The heaviest ice accumulation, generally between 1/4 and 3/4 of an inch, fell from Beatrice and Fairbury northwest toward Seward and also over western Iowa east through northeast of Omaha.  Sections of eastern Nebraska from around Lincoln to Fremont and Omaha experienced some icing, but accumulations were mostly under 1/4 inch across those locations.  Several thousand people lost power from the storm a few days late when the Christmas 2009 blizzard struck with 40+ mph winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated 1/4 to 3/4 of an inch of ice accumulation fell over much of the county.  When the winds picked up during the winter storm/blizzard that followed the ice storm, the ice covered power lines began whipping in the wind and tree limbs fell on power lines causing scattered power outages over the region. Norris Public Power reported that 2500 customers in a 5 county area, including many residents in Swanton, lost power from the storm.

Strong north winds of 30 to 40 mph with gusts to 50 mph created blizzard conditions and visibilities below a quarter mile through the afternoon and into the evening hours on December 25, 2009.  Snow drifts from 4 to 6 feet were common and some roads were closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense upper level low pressure area dropped southeast across the High Plains during the day, tightening the surface pressure gradient as it passed.  Northwest winds increased to 45-60 mph by mid-morning over much of northwest Kansas and southwest Nebraska.

An intense upper level low pressure area dropped southeast across the High Plains during the day, tightening the surface pressure gradient as it passed.  Northwest winds increased to 45-60 mph by mid-morning over much of northwest Kansas and southwest Nebraska.

An upper level disturbance on the west coast moved southward over the southern Rockies and then out onto the southern plains from approximately December 21st through the 26th. This system affected the Cheyenne CWA in two waves. The first occurred December 22 and 23rd and brought light snow with strong winds that created drifting snow and hazardous driving conditions. As the storm system moved eastward, it intensified and brought heavy snow and blizzard conditions to the central Plains over the Holiday weekend.  A second wave of significant winter weather occurred over southeast Wyoming and the western Nebraska panhandle in wraparound energy from late on the 24th through the 26th.  Heavy snow and very strong winds produced blizzard conditions in the northeast portions of the CWA with near blizzard conditions elsewhere. Many roads were closed, including interstates 80 and 25 on Christmas day. Two deaths were indirectly attributed to to the second wave of winter weather, one near Laramie, WY and the other near Guernsey, WY.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Union Pacific Railroad at Towers West (UP417) recorded sustained winds at or above 40 mph from 17:05MST to 19:10MST, with a maximum wind gust of 52 mph at 17:55MST. These high winds combined with recent snowfall produced extreme winter weather conditions during this time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense low pressure system in the Southern Plains lifted northward into Missouri on Thursday, December 24th, and into western Iowa on Friday, December 25th, 2009.  This resulted in moderate to heavy snowfall and blizzard conditions across much of central and north central Nebraska with strong north winds of 30 to 40 mph with gusts to 50 mph.  Most county highways and rural roads became closed during the morning of Friday, December 25th, as snowfall amounts ranged from 4 to 15 inches.  Many snow drifts ranged from 4 to 8 feet, with a few as high as 10 feet.  Persistent snowfall across the Pine Ridge in the northern Panhandle allowed snowfall amounts to range up to 36 inches in northern Sheridan County with snowdrifts from 3 to 10 feet and road closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from 5 to 8 inches across Keya Paha County.  Strong north winds of 30 to 40 mph with gusts to 50 mph created blizzard conditions and visibilities below a quarter mile through the afternoon and into the evening hours on December 25, 2009.  Snow drifts from 3 to 6 feet were common and many road closures were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front that moved across eastern Nebraska during the afternoon and evening of May 31st touched off a few severe thunderstorms in southeast Nebraska.
A warm front that moved across eastern Nebraska during the afternoon and evening of May 31st touched off a few severe thunderstorms in southeast Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny to nickel-size hail fell along with very heavy rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front that moved across eastern Nebraska during the afternoon and evening of May 31st touched off a few severe thunderstorms in southeast Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mostly pea-size but a few stones up to nickel-size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front which moved into southeast Nebraska during the morning of June 6th stalled from southeast Nebraska into central Kansas during the afternoon and evening of that day.  Temperatures by late afternoon climbed into the upper 80s and lower 90s south of the front while readings northwest of the front were in the 50s and 60s. Thunderstorms developed near the boundary over Kansas early in the evening, and developed or spread into southeast Nebraska during the mid evening hours.  The storms produced mainly large hail.

A strong low pressure system moved across the Central Plains on December 8th and 9th, 2009. Moderate to heavy snow accompanied this weather system from southwest Nebraska into eastern portions of north central Nebraska.  Snow began to fall Monday evening, December 7th, and continued into the early morning hours of Wednesday, December 9th.  Storm total snowfall amounts ranged from 5 to 10 inches with the heaviest amounts across Custer County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall reports from a Cooperative observer and the public ranged from 4 to 7 inches.  North winds of 15 to 30 mph caused some blowing and drifting with reduced visibility below a mile at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced marginally large hail near Dakota City in extreme northeast Nebraska late in the evening of June 7th.
A thunderstorm produced marginally large hail near Dakota City in extreme northeast Nebraska late in the evening of June 7th.

A front that had pushed through the area a few days earlier moved north as a warm front to near the Nebraska and Kansas border during the morning of May 15th. The front caused storms to develop over southeast Nebraska and at least one storm produced hail up to 3/4 inch in diameter.

Thunderstorms developed near and behind a cold front that pushed into northern Nebraska during the evening hours of May 21st.  Isolated large hail reports were received from the storms as they moved into far northeast Nebraska.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to nickel-size covered the ground east of Center.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds blew across parts of the Nebraska panhandle for a time with several strong gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong wind gust was recorded near Chadron State Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A sharp upper level trough shifted east out of the Rocky Mountains and across the Central Plains, bringing a round of heavy snow to south central Nebraska.  Lift present ahead of the main system started to bring snow to the area during the early morning hours, dropping a few inches of snow.  After a brief lull in the activity, snow increased once again during the afternoon as the main system moved into the area.  Snow continued to fall into the evening and overnight hours, and by the time it had come to an end the next morning, 6 to 12 inches had fallen across the area.  Along with the snowfall, strong northwest winds behind a passing surface cold front with sustained speeds near 30 mph and gusts near 45 mph caused blowing and drifting of the snow, as well as near white out conditions.  Due to the drifting, many roads became impassable.

An intense upper level low pressure system deepened over the Desert Southwest in the days preceding Christmas, and unleashed its fury across the plains on Christmas Day.  After a relative lull in activity during the daytime hours on Christmas Eve, conditions began to quickly deteriorate during the evening as the winter storm system reorganized across the Plains.  The system deepened across the Southern Plains and along the Missouri River, before shifting north and settling in across Iowa on Christmas Day.  Being oriented on the backside of the upper low pressure system allowed south central Nebraska to catch the brunt of the combination of snowfall and very strong winds associated with the storm.  While the entire area was affected by northwest winds of 40 mph, and gusts near 60 mph at times, the majority of the snow fell in an area generally north of Highway 6 and east of Highway 281.  In this area, Christmas Day snowfall amounts tallied 4 to 8 inches, with many 3 day storm totals reaching over a foot.  The snow and wind produced blizzard white-out conditions due to significant blowing and drifting of the snow.  Numerous roads became impassable and were closed, including major highways and Interstate 80 from Grand Island east to the Missouri River.   The timing and extent of the blizzard on Christmas led to cancellations or rescheduling of holiday religious services and festivities for those that needed to travel.  It took many days for city, residential and area rural roads to be cleared from this storm, and given the timing and widespread impacts, the Christmas Blizzard is expected to be remembered for many years.

A large upper level low pressure system brought a heavy wet snow to far southeast Nebraska.  The precipitation started as rain...mixed with then changed to snow before sunrise on the 16th and then mixed back with rain or changed over to rain later that afternoon.  However, before it did visibilities dropped to a 1/4 mile or less and snowfall rates of around 2 inches an hour were briefly reached.  Total snowfall of 6 to 10 inches was common along and to about 25 miles north of the Kansas border.  Higher amounts included 10 inches in Endicott and 8 inches at Diller and 5 miles northeast of Falls City.  The heavy wet snow caused numerous accidents and the weight of the snow downed tree branches onto power lines causing power losses to around 6,200 customers.  Schools were canceled or let out early on the 16th and several were still closed on the 17th.  Nebraska Emergency Management estimated that the storm caused more than $3.5 million in damages and a federal disaster declaration was approved for the counties involved in the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain changed to a wet heavy snow during the predawn hours of November 16th.  Snowfall rates through mid morning reached an inch or two per hour as visibilities dropped to a quarter mile or less.  Total snowfall was around 6 to 8 inches across most of the county, with 8 inches reported 5 miles northeast of Falls City among the highest reported in the county.  The water equivalent of the snow in Falls City was around 2 inches and this slushy heavy wet snow caused a few accidents and power losses in the morning as the weight of the snow downed large tree limbs which fell on power lines causing them to fall.  However, by afternoon the snow had mostly melted on roads.

Energy associated with a strong system moving out of the Desert Southwest brought a mixed bag of wintry precipitation to the area.  Through the morning hours, freezing drizzle, freezing rain, sleet and snow were observed across south central Nebraska.  Ice accumulations due to freezing rain were generally around one quarter of an inch, and confined to an area east of a line from Osceola to Red Cloud.  By late morning much of the precipitation had transitioned to snow, which then continued well into the afternoon and evening hours.  In the area which saw freezing rain early on, snowfall totals generally ranged from nothing to 2 inches, with the remainder of the area seeing 2 to 4 inches.

An intense upper level low pressure system deepened over the Desert Southwest in the days preceding Christmas, and unleashed its fury across the plains on Christmas Day.  After a relative lull in activity during the daytime hours on Christmas Eve, conditions began to quickly deteriorate during the evening as the winter storm system reorganized across the Plains. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense upper level low pressure system deepened over the Desert Southwest in the days preceding Christmas, and unleashed its fury across the plains on Christmas Day.  After a relative lull in activity during the daytime hours on Christmas Eve, conditions began to quickly deteriorate during the evening as the winter storm system reorganized across the Plains.  The system deepened across the Southern Plains and along the Missouri River, before shifting north and settling in across Iowa on Christmas Day.  Being oriented on the backside of the upper low pressure system allowed south central Nebraska to catch the brunt of the combination of snowfall and very strong winds associated with the storm.  While the entire area was affected by northwest winds of 40 mph, and gusts near 60 mph at times, the majority of the snow fell in an area generally north of Highway 6 and east of Highway 281.  In this area, Christmas Day snowfall amounts tallied 4 to 8 inches, with many 3 day storm totals reaching over a foot.  The snow and wind produced blizzard white-out conditions due to significant blowing and drifting of the snow.  Numerous roads became impassable and were closed, including major highways and Interstate 80 from Grand Island east to the Missouri River.   The timing and extent of the blizzard on Christmas led to cancellations or rescheduling of holiday religious services and festivities for those that needed to travel.  It took many days for city, residential and area rural roads to be cleared from this storm, and given the timing and widespread impacts, the Christmas Blizzard is expected to be remembered for many years.

A sharp upper level trough shifted east out of the Rocky Mountains and across the Central Plains, bringing a round of heavy snow to south central Nebraska.  Lift present ahead of the main system started to bring snow to the area during the early morning hours, dropping a few inches of snow.  After a brief lull in the activity, snow increased once again during the afternoon as the main system moved into the area.  Snow continued to fall into the evening and overnight hours, and by the time it had come to an end the next morning, 6 to 12 inches had fallen across the area.  Along with the snowfall, strong northwest winds behind a passing surface cold front with sustained speeds near 30 mph and gusts near 45 mph caused blowing and drifting of the snow, as well as near white out conditions.  Due to the drifting, many roads became impassable.

Energy associated with a strong system moving out of the Desert Southwest brought a mixed bag of wintry precipitation to the area.  Through the morning hours, freezing drizzle, freezing rain, sleet and snow were observed across south central Nebraska.  Ice accumulations due to freezing rain were generally around one quarter of an inch, and confined to an area east of a line from Osceola to Red Cloud.  By late morning much of the precipitation had transitioned to snow, which then continued well into the afternoon and evening hours.  In the area which saw freezing rain early on, snowfall totals generally ranged from nothing to 2 inches, with the remainder of the area seeing 2 to 4 inches.

An intense low pressure system in the Southern Plains lifted northward into Missouri on Thursday, December 24th, and into western Iowa on Friday, December 25th, 2009.  This resulted in moderate to heavy snowfall and blizzard conditions across much of central and north central Nebraska with strong north winds of 30 to 40 mph with gusts to 50 mph.  Most county highways and rural roads became closed during the morning of Friday, December 25th, as snowfall amounts ranged from 4 to 15 inches.  Many snow drifts ranged from 4 to 8 feet, with a few as high as 10 feet.  Persistent snowfall across the Pine Ridge in the northern Panhandle allowed snowfall amounts to range up to 36 inches in northern Sheridan County with snowdrifts from 3 to 10 feet and road closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from 4 to 6 inches across Logan County.  Strong north winds of 30 to 40 mph with gusts to 50 mph created blizzard conditions and visibilities below a quarter mile at times through the afternoon and into the early evening hours on December 25, 2009.  Snow drifts around 3 feet were common.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from 3 to 5 inches across Thomas County.  Strong north winds of 30 to 40 mph with gusts to 50 mph created blizzard conditions and visibilities below a quarter mile at times through the afternoon hours on December 25, 2009.  Snow drifts up to 3 feet were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level low pressure system brought a heavy wet snow to far southeast Nebraska.  The precipitation started as rain...mixed with then changed to snow before sunrise on the 16th and then mixed back with rain or changed over to rain later that afternoon.  However, before it did visibilities dropped to a 1/4 mile or less and snowfall rates of around 2 inches an hour were briefly reached.  Total snowfall of 6 to 10 inches was common along and to about 25 miles north of the Kansas border.  Higher amounts included 10 inches in Endicott and 8 inches at Diller and 5 miles northeast of Falls City.  The heavy wet snow caused numerous accidents and the weight of the snow downed tree branches onto power lines causing power losses to around 6,200 customers.  Schools were canceled or let out early on the 16th and several were still closed on the 17th.  Nebraska Emergency Management estimated that the storm caused more than $3.5 million in damages and a federal disaster declaration was approved for the counties involved in the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain changed to a wet heavy snow during the predawn hours of November 16th.  Snowfall rates through mid morning reached an inch or two per hour as visibilities dropped to a quarter mile or less.  Total snowfall was 6 to 10 inches across most of the central and southern sections of the county.  Higher amounts included 10 inches in Endicott, 8 inches at Diller and 6 inches in Fairbury.  The slushy heavy wet snow caused several accidents and power losses in the morning but by afternoon it had mostly melted on roads.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from 3 to 5 inches across McPherson County. Snow drifts from 1 to 3 feet were common.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance on the west coast moved southward over the southern Rockies and then out onto the southern plains from approximately December 21st through the 26th. This system affected the Cheyenne CWA in two waves. The first occurred December 22 and 23rd and brought light snow with strong winds that created drifting snow and hazardous driving conditions. As the storm system moved eastward, it intensified and brought heavy snow and blizzard conditions to the central Plains over the Holiday weekend.  A second wave of significant winter weather occurred over southeast Wyoming and the western Nebraska panhandle in wraparound energy from late on the 24th through the 26th.  Heavy snow and very strong winds produced blizzard conditions in the northeast portions of the CWA with near blizzard conditions elsewhere. Many roads were closed, including interstates 80 and 25 on Christmas day. Two deaths were indirectly attributed to to the second wave of winter weather, one near Laramie, WY and the other near Guernsey, WY.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions were seen across the northern Nebraska panhandle through Christmas day and continued through the following morning. Area roads were closed due to snow and blowing snow reducing visibilities and creating hazardous driving conditions. 1455 MST, a trained spotter in Harrison reported visibility a few hundred yards since the late morning with 3 ft drifts piling up. At 1832 MST, a county official in Harrison reported continued low visibility of 300 ft or less in snow and blowing snow.

ASOS at Chadron Municipal Airport reported visibilities at 1/4 SM beginning at 1143 MST. At 1244 MST, local law enforcement in Chadron reported an estimated 4 to 6 inches of snow had fallen. Another report came in at approximately 1500 MST with drifts 3-4 ft high due to extremely windy conditions. At 2015 MST, a county official in Chadron reported continued low visibilities of 1/4 SM since around noon. At 2330 MST, local law enforcement in Chadron reported continuing visibilities less than 1/4 mile due to strong winds and snow in town. At this time, the ASOS at Chadron Municipal Airport was reporting 3/4 SM with wind gusts of 35 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced marginally large hail near Ponca in extreme northeast Nebraska during the predawn hours on September 21st.
An isolated thunderstorm produced some hail around Bridgeport, Nebraska.
A cold front produced some thunderstorms over parts of the Nebraska panhandle.
A cold front produced some thunderstorms over parts of the Nebraska panhandle.
Thunderstorms produced heavy rain, which caused flash flooding of roads in Dixon County from just before to a little midnight on August 15th and 16th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from thunderstorms caused flash flooding of a few roads, including U.S. Highway 20.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms, a few severe, developed along a cold front that pushed slowly into southeast Nebraska during the mid to late afternoon hours of August 9th.  Temperatures ahead of the front climbed into the 90s with dew points in the 70s providing a modestly unstable environment.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to 2 inches in diameter caused damage to at least one vehicle in the area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter estimated that thunderstorm wind gusts of around 65 mph downed 2 to 3 inch diameter tree limbs.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated at around 60 mph downed 4 inch diameter tree limbs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms caused damaging winds and flash flood producing heavy rains in Dakota County in extreme northeast Nebraska from a little before to a little after midnight on August 24th and 25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused tree damage. A large tree on the west side of the town of Jackson was blown down across a road, and trees up to 5 inches in diameter were broken off in the town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms caused damaging winds and flash flood producing heavy rains in Dakota County in extreme northeast Nebraska from a little before to a little after midnight on August 24th and 25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding of streets in the towns of South Sioux City and Dakota City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broken line of thunderstorms developed from south central into east central Nebraska during the early morning hours of September 21.  At least one of the storms produced large hail before they weakened by the time they made it into the Lincoln and Omaha areas.
An isolated thunderstorm produced heavy rain and some hail over parts of Kimball county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail size ranged from 3/4 to 1 inch in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated thunderstorm produced heavy rain and some hail over parts of Kimball county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding caused some washouts on county road 14.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and fast moving clipper system produced high winds across western and north central Nebraska on January 12, 2009.  Northwest winds of 35 to 45 mph with gusts to 60 mph occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts near 60 mph occurred across Custer County during the late morning and early afternoon hours. A peak wind gust of 58 mph occurred at 1151 am CST at the Broken Bow, Nebraska, ASOS site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and fast moving clipper system produced high winds across western and north central Nebraska on January 12, 2009.  Northwest winds of 35 to 45 mph with gusts to 60 mph occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts near 60 mph occurred across Deuel County during the morning and early afternoon hours. A peak wind gust of 58 mph occurred at 0850 am MST at the Big Springs, Nebraska, mesonet site. Winds gusts to 60 mph occurred at the Sidney, Nebraska, ASOS site just west of Deuel County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and fast moving clipper system produced high winds across western and north central Nebraska on January 12, 2009.  Northwest winds of 35 to 45 mph with gusts to 60 mph occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very strong winds of 50 to 60 mph buffeted the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Arctic air mass invaded southeast Wyoming on December 9th and combined with strong winds to produce extremely cold wind chills on the morning of December 9th and again through the night into December 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The AWOS at Alliance Municipal Airport (KAIA) reported a minimum wind chill of -43F at 0353 LST with values below -30F since 0153 LST. Wind chill values had been below -20F since 9/1553 LST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving low pressure area brought six to ten inches of snow across extreme northwest Kansas, southwest Nebraska and eastern Yuma county Colorado.  Winds of 15 to 25 mph produced occasional blowing snow and reduced visibility.  Highest snow totals in southwest Nebraska included 7 inches at Benkelman, 9-10 inches in extreme northern Dundy County, 10 inches north of Stratton, 5 inches in Culbertson, 7 inches northwest of McCook and 6 inches at Indianola.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts included 5.8 inches near Trenton, 10 inches north of Stratton and 5 inches at Culbertson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds of 35 to 45 mph with gusts to around 60 mph, combined with snow to create low visibilities in snow and blowing snow across eastern Cherry, Keya Paha, and Brown County during the late morning and early afternoon hours on January 12, 2009. The low visibilities, occasionally near zero, created hazardous travel conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very strong winds of 35 to 45 mph with gusts around 60 mph caused low visibilities in snow and blowing snow.  Occasionally, visibilities dropped to near zero creating hazardous travel conditions. The Rose mesonet site recorded a wind gust of 66 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large and relatively slow moving storm brought a prolonged winter storm and even, for a time, blizzard conditions to eastern Nebraska and western Iowa from late on Dec. 7th through the early morning hours of Dec. 9th.  The storm was driven by an upper level disturbance that tracked from the west coast across the Rockies and deepened over the Mississippi River Valley.  The heaviest snow fell in advance of when the stronger winds arrived, mainly during the morning and afternoon of Dec. 8th.  However, as north winds increased to 30 to 50 mph during the night of the 8th and early on the 9th, visibilities frequently dropped to near zero, especially in open areas.  Considerable drifting snow also occurred, in many cases closing roads back shut almost as fast as they could be opened.  This prompted many counties to pull snow plows off the roads for a while during the night of the 8th and early on the 9th.  Many schools were closed for 3 days because of the storm; due the forecast of heavy snow to begin on the 7th, because of poor visibilities and roads that were drifted shut on the 8th and still some closed roads and bitter cold wind chills which followed the storm on the 9th.  An elderly Omaha man was found dead during the evening of the 8th when he apparently had car trouble and returned to his apartment and was found dead sitting down in a chair outside. ||Total snowfall from the storm was 6 to 15 inches over most of eastern Nebraska and western Iowa.  Higher amounts in eastern Nebraska included 15 inches in Union, 14 inches in Columbus, around 12 inches at the NWS in Valley, at Tekamah, Uehling, Steele City and Weeping Water.  In western Iowa heavier amounts included 12 inches in Clarinda and Hastings and around 11 inches in Harlan, Red Oak and Little Sioux.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 8 to 14 inches fell across much of the county from this storm with 14 inches measured in Columbus and around 9 inches in Humphrey. The snow began during the late evening of 12/7 and continued into the early morning hours of 12/9.  Winds were not that strong when the snow started, but north winds increased during the afternoon and evening of the 8th and into the next morning.  Frequent wind gusts of 40 mph or more were observed with a peak gust of 47 mph measures by the AWOS in Columbus.  The winds produced considerable blowing and drifting snow with blizzard conditions during the evening and early morning hours of the 8th and 9th. Severe drifting of snow also closed many county roads including sections of Highway 81 and 91 north of Columbus.  It was estimated that snow removal costs in Columbus alone would be around $40,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second winter storm of the month hit eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa as a complex weather pattern brought a prolonged period of winter weather, including blizzard conditions, to the region around Christmas.  Low pressure aloft in the southern plains lifted northeast into Missouri as another low pressure system dropped south out of Canada.  These two systems then merged over the central United States and eventually pulled Atlantic moisture westward into the plains.  Before they merged, the southern system pulled up gulf moisture and brought areas of freezing rain to southeast Nebraska and 3 to 5 inches of snow to northeast Nebraska on 12/23.  The second system pulled down Arctic Air as north winds gusted between 40 and 50 mph over most of the region.  This not only changed all the precipitation to snow on the 24th, but also brought blizzard conditions to much of eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa Christmas Eve and much of Christmas Day as the systems merged.  Snow and blowing snow and occasional blizzard or near-blizzard conditions then continued through much of the 26th.||Snowfall from the prolonged winter storm was 10 to 18 inches over most of eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa.  Heavier totals included around 18 inches in Norfolk, around 16 inches in Neligh and near Little Sioux Iowa, around 15 inches in Columbus, near Verdel Nebraska and Shenandoah Iowa, 14 inches near Gretna, Bennington and Logan Iowa and 13 inches at the NWS in Valley, near Auburn, Fremont, Ft. Calhoun, Wayne, Bloomfield, and Papillion.  The snow and strong winds drifted most rural roads shut and even made many highways impassable, especially in northeast Nebraska and sections of southeast Nebraska northwest through southwest of Lincoln where winds were a bit stronger.  President Obama granted disaster declarations for many counties hit by this storm and the one in early January.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A prolonged winter storm/blizzard hit the area closing many roads and highways and bringing most traffic to a standstill for a few days.  Snowfall from the storm began Christmas Eve morning and lasted well into the 26th with substantial blowing and drifting snow and occasional periods of accumulating snow finally tapering off around midnight on the 27th.  Northwest winds of 25 to 35 mph with gusts of  40 mph occurred for an extended period during the height of the storm. Total snowfall from the storm was estimated at 10 to 14 inches over most of the county with 13 inches measured in Wayne among the highest reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A compact upper level system that tracked from the northern Rockies across central Nebraska and into eastern Kansas brought a 12 to 18 hour period of snow to eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa.  Although the snow was briefly heavy, water equivalent was generally fairly low and most accumulations over the region were less than 6 inches.  However, isolated locations received 6 or more inches, especially across Pierce county where 6 to 9 inches fell over much of the county.  Even though north winds occasionally gusted over 20 mph, blowing and drifting snow was not a major issue with this storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 6 to 6.5 inches was reported by observers east through north of Pierce and one 9 inch amount was measured by a CoCoRaHS observer 9 miles west of Pierce.
