Northwest winds of 25 to 35 mph with gusts over 40 mph occurred for an extended period during the height of the storm.  A peak gust of 45 mph was recorded by the Asos in Tekamah.  Total snowfall from the storm was estimated at 8 to 16 inches over most of the county based on observations from surrounding towns and 8 inches measured in Tekamah.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second winter storm of the month hit eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa as a complex weather pattern brought a prolonged period of winter weather, including blizzard conditions, to the region around Christmas.  Low pressure aloft in the southern plains lifted northeast into Missouri as another low pressure system dropped south out of Canada.  These two systems then merged over the central United States and eventually pulled Atlantic moisture westward into the plains.  Before they merged, the southern system pulled up gulf moisture and brought areas of freezing rain to southeast Nebraska and 3 to 5 inches of snow to northeast Nebraska on 12/23.  The second system pulled down Arctic Air as north winds gusted between 40 and 50 mph over most of the region.  This not only changed all the precipitation to snow on the 24th, but also brought blizzard conditions to much of eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa Christmas Eve and much of Christmas Day as the systems merged.  Snow and blowing snow and occasional blizzard or near-blizzard conditions then continued through much of the 26th.||Snowfall from the prolonged winter storm was 10 to 18 inches over most of eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa.  Heavier totals included around 18 inches in Norfolk, around 16 inches in Neligh and near Little Sioux Iowa, around 15 inches in Columbus, near Verdel Nebraska and Shenandoah Iowa, 14 inches near Gretna, Bennington and Logan Iowa and 13 inches at the NWS in Valley, near Auburn, Fremont, Ft. Calhoun, Wayne, Bloomfield, and Papillion.  The snow and strong winds drifted most rural roads shut and even made many highways impassable, especially in northeast Nebraska and sections of southeast Nebraska northwest through southwest of Lincoln where winds were a bit stronger.  President Obama granted disaster declarations for many counties hit by this storm and the one in early January.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A prolonged winter storm/blizzard hit the area closing many roads and highways and bringing most traffic to a standstill for a few days. Northwest winds of 25 to 35 mph with gusts over 40 mph occurred for an extended period during the height of the storm.  Total snowfall from the storm was estimated at 12 to 16 inches over most of the county with around 13 inches measured in Ft. Calhoun among the highest reported.  The Blair city administrator commented that it was one of the worst storms to hit the area in the last 30 to 40 years and it produced drifts over 15 feet in rural areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense upper level low pressure system deepened over the Desert Southwest in the days preceding Christmas, and unleashed its fury across the plains on Christmas Day.  After a relative lull in activity during the daytime hours on Christmas Eve, conditions began to quickly deteriorate during the evening as the winter storm system reorganized across the Plains.  The system deepened across the Southern Plains and along the Missouri River, before shifting north and settling in across Iowa on Christmas Day.  Being oriented on the backside of the upper low pressure system allowed south central Nebraska to catch the brunt of the combination of snowfall and very strong winds associated with the storm.  While the entire area was affected by northwest winds of 40 mph, and gusts near 60 mph at times, the majority of the snow fell in an area generally north of Highway 6 and east of Highway 281.  In this area, Christmas Day snowfall amounts tallied 4 to 8 inches, with many 3 day storm totals reaching over a foot.  The snow and wind produced blizzard white-out conditions due to significant blowing and drifting of the snow.  Numerous roads became impassable and were closed, including major highways and Interstate 80 from Grand Island east to the Missouri River.   The timing and extent of the blizzard on Christmas led to cancellations or rescheduling of holiday religious services and festivities for those that needed to travel.  It took many days for city, residential and area rural roads to be cleared from this storm, and given the timing and widespread impacts, the Christmas Blizzard is expected to be remembered for many years.

A sharp upper level trough shifted east out of the Rocky Mountains and across the Central Plains, bringing a round of heavy snow to south central Nebraska.  Lift present ahead of the main system started to bring snow to the area during the early morning hours, dropping a few inches of snow.  After a brief lull in the activity, snow increased once again during the afternoon as the main system moved into the area.  Snow continued to fall into the evening and overnight hours, and by the time it had come to an end the next morning, 6 to 12 inches had fallen across the area.  Along with the snowfall, strong northwest winds behind a passing surface cold front with sustained speeds near 30 mph and gusts near 45 mph caused blowing and drifting of the snow, as well as near white out conditions.  Due to the drifting, many roads became impassable.

Along with the snowfall, strong northwest winds behind a passing surface cold front with sustained speeds near 30 mph and gusts near 45 mph caused blowing and drifting of the snow, as well as near white out conditions.  Due to the drifting, many roads became impassable.

An intense upper level low pressure system deepened over the Desert Southwest in the days preceding Christmas, and unleashed its fury across the plains on Christmas Day.  After a relative lull in activity during the daytime hours on Christmas Eve, conditions began to quickly deteriorate during the evening as the winter storm system reorganized across the Plains.  The system deepened across the Southern Plains and along the Missouri River, before shifting north and settling in across Iowa on Christmas Day.  Being oriented on the backside of the upper low pressure system allowed south central Nebraska to catch the brunt of the combination of snowfall and very strong winds associated with the storm.  While the entire area was affected by northwest winds of 40 mph, and gusts near 60 mph at times, the majority of the snow fell in an area generally north of Highway 6 and east of Highway 281.  In this area, Christmas Day snowfall amounts tallied 4 to 8 inches, with many 3 day storm totals reaching over a foot.  The snow and wind produced blizzard white-out conditions due to significant blowing and drifting of the snow.  Numerous roads became impassable and were closed, including major highways and Interstate 80 from Grand Island east to the Missouri River.   The timing and extent of the blizzard on Christmas led to cancellations or rescheduling of holiday religious services and festivities for those that needed to travel.  It took many days for city, residential and area rural roads to be cleared from this storm, and given the timing and widespread impacts, the Christmas Blizzard is expected to be remembered for many years.

Due to the drifting, many roads became impassable.

A stout surface cold front moved south through the Central Plains during the morning hours of the 12th, with strong high pressure building in across the region behind the front.  Along with impressive pressure rises behind the front, stronger winds aloft were able to mix down to the surface, bringing northwest winds which increased during the afternoon hours to near 40 mph, with gusts of 50 to 60 mph.  There were a few reports of brief power outages from lines touching, along with one semi truck accident.

An upper level storm system tracked from Colorado across Northern Kansas on February 13th, 2009.  Heavy snow with amounts of 6 to 10 inches resulted across west central and central Nebraska by late morning. Heavy snowfall rates of 2 to 3 inches per hour were observed between 6 an 9 am CST across portions of Lincoln and Custer County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow with amounts ranging from 6 to 10 inches occurred across Lincoln County during the morning hours on February 13th. Snowfall of 10 inches was reported 15 miles north of North Platte. Snowfall of 8.3 inches was observed at the North Platte regional airport.  Heavy snowfall rates of two inches per hours were observed between 6 am and 9 am CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level system tracked from Western Kansas into Eastern Nebraska.  This brought a 100 mile wide band of moderate to heavy snowfall from the southeastern Nebraska Panhandle across the eastern half of north central Nebraska on March 7-8, 2009. The heaviest snowfall occurred across Arthur county, northeast across Hooker, southeastern Cherry, and southern Brown county.  These counties reported snowfall amounts ranging from 6 to as much as 14 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall occurred across McPherson county during the late afternoon and evening hours on March 7, 2009. Snowfall amounts of 4 to 8 inches were reported across the county with the heaviest amounts near 8 inches across the northwest portion of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense low pressure that tracked from southeast South Dakota into Iowa brought strong winds...snow and areas of zero visibilities in blowing snow to much of eastern Nebraska.  Especially hard hit was northeast Nebraska where many highways were closed during the afternoon and evening due to blizzard or near blizzard conditions, with numerous accidents reported.  In addition, several schools in northeast Nebraska didn't dismiss students and only released them if they were picked up by parents or a designee.  Morning temperatures that were in the 30s and even some lower 40s fell into the teens by afternoon as northwest winds gusted between 45 and 58 mph over much of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions were observed over much of Knox county most of the afternoon of 1/12/09.  Snow...strong winds...falling temperatures and whiteout conditions were reported.  Even though new snowfall was only an inch or two, the blowing and drifting snow and poor visibilities closed many roads and highways in northeast Nebraska.  Numerous accidents were reported and many other vehicles pulled off to the side of the road for several hours to ride out the storm.  Several schools in the area also did not observe a normal dismissal, only releasing students to parents or a designee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked from western Nebraska into northern Minnesota from the morning of February 9th through the morning of the 10th.  The low brought very strong south winds to eastern Nebraska during the afternoon into the early evening hours of February 9th with sustained winds of 30 to 35 mph and some gusts as high as 65 mph.  The prolonged nature of the sustained strong winds caused scattered damage across an area from around Fairbury north to near Columbus.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind equipment maintained by emergency management near Fairbury measured a wind gust of 65 mph.  The strong winds downed isolated power lines and knocked down a few 2-3 inch diameter tree branches and caused other isolated damage in the area. The winds were from the south and caused by low pressure tracking north of the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early spring storm brought strong winds and near-blizzard conditions to sections of northeast Nebraska the last day of March 2009.  An upper level low pressure system lifted from western Kansas across eastern Nebraska causing a surface low to quickly lift from Kansas across northeast Nebraska and into southern Minnesota.  Although snowfall was only 1 to 3 inches, north winds that gusted to 50 mph caused substantial blowing and drifting snow with visibilities frequently reduced to a 1/2 mile or less in open areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 1 to 3 inches was accompanied by north winds of 30 to 35 mph with gusts of 50 to 55 mph.  This produced significant blowing and drifting snow over the county closing many roads and frequently reducing visibilities below 1/2 mile.  Around 3 inches of snow from this storm was reported in Bloomfield for one of the higher amounts reported in the county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Although snowfall from this storm across Antelope county was only an inch or two, it was accompanied by north winds of 30 to 35 mph with gusts of 50 to 55 mph. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong early season cold front brought snow to extreme southeast Wyoming and the southern Nebraska panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter measured 8.5 inches of snow in Sidney.|At 18:00MST a COOP observer in Dalton reported 8 inches of snow.|At 20:00MST a COOP observer in Lodgepole measured 8 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong early season cold front brought snow to extreme southeast Wyoming and the southern Nebraska panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A COOP observer located 15 miles south of Bushnell reported 9 inches of snow.|At 20:00MST a COOP observer located 2 miles northeast of Kimball reported 6 inches of snow.|At 20:00MST law enforcement officials in Kimball reported an estimated 6 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense upper level low pressure system tracked from eastern Colorado into southeast South Dakota from March 23rd into March 24th.  This caused a surface low pressure system to move from western Nebraska into southeast South Dakota during that time.  As the low tracked northeast, a dry-line moved into eastern Nebraska during the afternoon of March 23rd.  With surface temperatures in the 60s and 70s, and dewpoint temperatures in the 50s, ample instability was in place to allow a line of severe thunderstorms to develop as the dry-line punched eastward.  Several fast moving low-topped supercell thunderstorms were embedded in the line, and one cell produced cyclic tornadoes from southeast of Lincoln into western Iowa.  The storms in the line were moving north northeast at 50 to 60 mph.  In addition to the severe weather, strong southerly gradient winds prevailed ahead of the low pressure.  In some cases the winds gusted between 50 and 65 mph for several hours across parts of eastern Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered wind damage from a low-topped supercell thunderstorm that earlier produced 5 tornadoes to the south, was reported west through southwest of the South Bend area.  Due to the tree and farm outbuilding damage noted, a NWS Storm Survey estimated that wind speeds were between 90 and 100 mph.

Several fast moving low-topped supercell thunderstorms were embedded in the line, and one cell produced cyclic tornadoes from southeast of Lincoln into western Iowa.  The storms in the line were moving north northeast at 50 to 60 mph.  In addition to the severe weather, strong southerly gradient winds prevailed ahead of the low pressure. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball size hail was reported around 5 miles south of the southern part of Lincoln.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful low pressure system deepened over eastern Colorado and moved across Nebraska, producing blizzard conditions over much of the Nebraska panhandle.  Numerous roads were closed due to blowing and drifting snow and extremely low visibilities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds with gusts in excess of 50 mph combined with an estimated 3 to 6 inches of snowfall to produce blizzard conditions with drifts in excess of 3 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and slow moving spring storm caused severe thunderstorms to occur across north central Nebraska. As colder was drawn into the system, strong winds and snow combined to produce blizzard conditions across the western half of north central Nebraska.  Snowfall amounts ranged from 3 to 8 inches with snow drifts ranging up to 5 feet across Sheridan county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow and strong northwest winds of 30 to 40 mph with gusts to around 60 mph occurred during the afternoon and overnight hours.  Valentine Nebraska ASOS reported surface visibility a quarter mile or less during the overnight hours into the morning hours on March 24th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong spring storm system brought heavy snowfall of 3 to 8 inches along with strong northwest winds of 30 to 40 mph with gusts over 50 mph to portions of north central Nebraska. This brought winter storm and blizzard conditions to much of north central Nebraska during the overnight hours on March 30th, through the early afternoon hours on March 31st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest winds of 25 to 35 mph combined with moderate to heavy snow to produce snowfall amounts of 1 to 5 inches and surface visibility of a quarter mile or less at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong, slow moving upper level low pressure system shifted east out of the Rocky Mountains and onto the plains during the daytime hours of March 23rd.  A well vertically stacked system, it brought strong winds as well as thunderstorms to portions of South Central Nebraska.  With strong forcing and a tight pressure gradient across the area, this lead to widespread sustained wind speeds of 35 to 40 mph and gusts near 60 mph, which lingered into the evening hours.  These winds blew some dust around, causing some problems for travelers, also making it difficult to fight a number of wildfires which developed across the area.  Some strong to severe thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours, dropping penny to half dollar size hail across eastern portions of the area, with some damage reported in Hamilton and Fillmore Counties.

An intense upper level low pressure system tracked from eastern Colorado into southeast South Dakota from March 23rd into March 24th.  This caused a surface low pressure system to move from western Nebraska into southeast South Dakota during that time.  As the low tracked northeast, a dry-line moved into eastern Nebraska during the afternoon of March 23rd.  With surface temperatures in the 60s and 70s, and dewpoint temperatures in the 50s, ample instability was in place to allow a line of severe thunderstorms to develop as the dry-line punched eastward.  Several fast moving low-topped supercell thunderstorms were embedded in the line, and one cell produced cyclic tornadoes from southeast of Lincoln into western Iowa.  The storms in the line were moving north northeast at 50 to 60 mph.  In addition to the severe weather, strong southerly gradient winds prevailed ahead of the low pressure.  In some cases the winds gusted between 50 and 65 mph for several hours across parts of eastern Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of 35 to 40 mph with gusts of 60 to 65 mph prevailed over the county from late morning through early evening.  Several weather recording stations reported wind gusts of 60 to 65 mph including one maintained by emergency management which recorded a 65 mph wind gust at 258 pm CDT.  The strong winds downed trees and large limbs, power lines, caused some building damage in Fairbury and nearby Endicott and created very poor visibilities in blowing dust.  The winds also flipped 2 semis.  One semi was overturned southwest of Fairbury around 240 pm CDT and the other was blown over 1/2 mile east of Jansen on Highway 136 around 320 pm.  The second resulted in minor injuries to the driver.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated at 60 to 65 mph tipped over 3 semi-trailer on Highway 2 from around 4 pm until 5 pm CDT.  The winds also destroyed a garage and snapped several pine trees near Palmyra around 630 pm.  The winds were from gradient south or southwest winds that blew in advance of and behind a line of thunderstorms that developed west of region and moved across the region.  The semis were tipped generally in advance of the storms, while the garage and tree damage occurred with the line 10 or 20 miles to the east.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated at close to 60 mph blew a storage shed located at the Tecumseh Livestock Auction into a power pole along Calhoun Street.  This caused power to be lost for around 45 minutes for about half the residents of Tecumseh, with a few customers without power for a day or two.  The winds were from gradient south winds that blew in advance of thunderstorms that developed west of region.  When the damage occurred, the line of thunderstorms was still about 20 to 30 miles to the west and moving north northeast.

A semi trailer was also overturned near Dorchester.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pressure gradient produced a brief period of strong winds over parts of the Nebraska panhandle, with particularly strong wind gusts around Kimball, Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gust recorded at the Kimball airport.  One semi-truck was blown over just north of Kimball.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low pressure system moving out of the desert southwest and crossing the central and southern plains brought snow starting during the early morning hours of the 13th.  As this system shifted through Kansas, this placed portions of south central Nebraska in an area of enhanced lift, allowing for the accumulation of several inches of snow.  Winds generally remained between 10 and 20 mph, with gusts near 25 to 30 mph at times, and combined with the at times heavy snow, caused periodic reductions in visibility down to a half to a quarter of a mile.  Locations generally along and north of Interstate 80 saw the highest snow accumulations, with numerous reports of 6 to 9 inches.  South of Interstate 80, accumulations were slightly lower, ranging from 4 to 7 inches.  This storm caused the closing of many schools and the postponement of events across the area.

An intense upper low that tracked along the Kansas and Nebraska border and resultant surface low pressure system helped spawn a few severe thunderstorms, and even a tornado, over southeast Nebraska.  The storms developed near a warm front that extended from the low into southeast Nebraska.  Surface temperatures south of the warm front climbed into the 60s and 70s but dewpoint temperatures both ahead of and just behind the warm front managed to only climb into the 30s and 40s, somewhat limiting the coverage of the severe thunderstorms.  This system produced blizzard conditions and areas of heavy snow from the Nebraska Sandhills into northeast Nebraska during the afternoon and overnight hours of April 4th and 5th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management spotted possibly two funnel clouds southeast of the the Jct of Highways 41 and 77 south of Cortland.  The funnels were from the same mini-supercell thunderstorm that produced a tornado north of Beatrice.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Supercell storms developed across western and north central Nebraska during the afternoon of June 17th and produced very large hail, heavy rain, funnels, and a brief tornado.  As the evening progressed, the storms transitioned into bow echoes and produced strong straight-line winds during the early morning hours of June 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail broke windows and damaged the siding on a home.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail broke windows and piled up to 3 inches deep.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The same strong storm system which caused severe weather across portions of the region on April 4th, brought a round of winter weather to portions of South Central Nebraska during the overnight hours and into the first half of the day on April 5th.  This strong low pressure system tracked near the Nebraska and Kansas state line, with strong winds and snow on the northern edge.  The area which saw the most impact was along and north of Interstate 80, where peak winds of 45 to 55 mph were reported.  Such strong winds combined with the falling snow made travel very difficult to impossible at times, with visibilities reduces down to less than one quarter of a mile.  With the blowing and drifting of the snow, taking measurements after the storm was quite challenging, but many snowfall totals of 2 to 6 inches were reported.  In anticipation of the storm, many towns across the area declared snow emergencies.

A cold front that touched off isolated severe thunderstorms on April 24th and early on the 25th stalled over northeast Kansas and northwest Missouri on the 25th.  The front then returned north as a warm front during the morning of April 26th.  Temperatures south of the front climbed into the 70s with 40s and 50s prevailing to the north.  This contrast, combined with an upper level disturbance, helped kick off a line of thunderstorms north of the returning warm front.  The storms produced large hail as far north as the Omaha area and some damaging wind gusts in southwest Iowa east and southeast of Omaha.  In addition, with the storms tracking across the same area for many hours, some heavy rain fell over far southeast Nebraska and southwest Iowa.  Doppler Radar estimated and cooperative observers reported that some locations received 3 inches or more for 24 hour rainfall amounts from the storm.  However, no significant flooding was reported.

Portions of South Central Nebraska saw another round of thunderstorms just prior to midnight, as yet another upper level disturbance moved across the region.  A few strong to severe thunderstorms developed, with nickel size hail reported in Nuckolls County, and penny to golf ball size hail reported in Thayer County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny to quarter size hail reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An ice jam that briefly formed upstream of North Bend caused flooding along the Platte River around the McGinn Lake area several miles west of North Bend.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ice jam about 3/4 mile in length formed about 1 to 2 miles upstream of North Bend.  The ice jam caused flooding along the Platte River in the vicinity of McGinns Lake area.  The flooding surrounded several lake homes and flooded a few rooms of at least one home.

Such strong winds combined with the falling snow made travel very difficult to impossible at times, with visibilities reduces down to less than one quarter of a mile.  With the blowing and drifting of the snow, taking measurements after the storm was quite challenging, but many snowfall totals of 2 to 6 inches were reported.  In anticipation of the storm, many towns across the area declared snow emergencies.

A cold front that touched off isolated severe thunderstorms on April 24th and early on the 25th stalled over northeast Kansas and northwest Missouri on the 25th.  The front then returned north as a warm front during the morning of April 26th.  Temperatures south of the front climbed into the 70s with 40s and 50s prevailing to the north.  This contrast, combined with an upper level disturbance, helped kick off a line of thunderstorms north of the returning warm front.  The storms produced large hail as far north as the Omaha area and some damaging wind gusts in southwest Iowa east and southeast of Omaha.  In addition, with the storms tracking across the same area for many hours, some heavy rain fell over far southeast Nebraska and southwest Iowa.  Doppler Radar estimated and cooperative observers reported that some locations received 3 inches or more for 24 hour rainfall amounts from the storm.  However, no significant flooding was reported.

Supercell storms developed across western and north central Nebraska during the afternoon of June 17th and produced very large hail, heavy rain, funnels, and a brief tornado.  As the evening progressed, the storms transitioned into bow echoes and produced strong straight-line winds during the early morning hours of June 18th.

As the evening progressed, the storms transitioned into bow echoes and produced strong straight-line winds during the early morning hours of June 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The winds overturned center pivot irrigation systems.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening of June 18th and early morning hours of June 19th, supercell storm developed over western Nebraska and produced strong winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported 60 mph winds along with the hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense upper level low pressure system tracked from eastern Colorado into southeast South Dakota from March 23rd into March 24th.  This caused a surface low pressure system to move from western Nebraska into southeast South Dakota during that time.  As the low tracked northeast, a dry-line moved into eastern Nebraska during the afternoon of March 23rd.  With surface temperatures in the 60s and 70s, and dewpoint temperatures in the 50s, ample instability was in place to allow a line of severe thunderstorms to develop as the dry-line punched eastward.  Several fast moving low-topped supercell thunderstorms were embedded in the line, and one cell produced cyclic tornadoes from southeast of Lincoln into western Iowa.  The storms in the line were moving north northeast at 50 to 60 mph.  In addition to the severe weather, strong southerly gradient winds prevailed ahead of the low pressure.  In some cases the winds gusted between 50 and 65 mph for several hours across parts of eastern Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This is the fifth and last of 5 cyclic tornadoes that a low-topped supercell thunderstorm produced at it tracked southeast through east of the Lincoln area.  This tornado touched down in Alvo and tracked a little over 10 miles north northeast before lifting just west of South Bend.  The tornado knocked down power poles and transmission lines, snapped trees and damaged houses and destroyed farm out buildings along its path.  One of the downed power lines fell on a car trapping its occupant for several hours.  Several cars were crushed by fallen trees in the Alvo area.  From the 3 confirmed tornadoes that hit the county, plus any associated wind damage, emergency management estimated that 30 homes had minor damage with 4 sustaining major damage and 60 to 80 farm outbuildings were damaged with the total damage approaching $1 million.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance and associated surface low pressure system tracked along the Nebraska and Kansas border spreading a mix of freezing rain...sleet and snow to sections of northeast Nebraska.  The system pulled down arctic air behind causing temperatures to plunge to the single digits and north winds to increase to 20 to 30 mph or greater.  The combination of mixed precipitation and strong winds caused scattered power outages in northeast Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain fell across the area for a while during the evening of March 9th followed by plunging temperatures and gusty North winds as an arctic air mass plunged into the region.  Although most ice accumulations were under 1/4 inch, the combination of ice and wind gusts of 40 mph or more caused scattered power outages from around Battle Creek to Elgin.  Ten sub-stations were knocked down due to the ice-wind combination causing 3500 customers to lose power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong, slow moving upper level low pressure system shifted east out of the Rocky Mountains and onto the plains during the daytime hours of March 23rd.  A well vertically stacked system, it brought strong winds as well as thunderstorms to portions of South Central Nebraska.  With strong forcing and a tight pressure gradient across the area, this lead to widespread sustained wind speeds of 35 to 40 mph and gusts near 60 mph, which lingered into the evening hours.  These winds blew some dust around, causing some problems for travelers, also making it difficult to fight a number of wildfires which developed across the area.  Some strong to severe thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours, dropping penny to half dollar size hail across eastern portions of the area, with some damage reported in Hamilton and Fillmore Counties.

With strong forcing and a tight pressure gradient across the area, this lead to widespread sustained wind speeds of 35 to 40 mph and gusts near 60 mph, which lingered into the evening hours.  These winds blew some dust around, causing some problems for travelers, also making it difficult to fight a number of wildfires which developed across the area.  Some strong to severe thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours, dropping penny to half dollar size hail across eastern portions of the area, with some damage reported in Hamilton and Fillmore Counties.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous hail reports received from the Trenton area in a 20 minute period. Reports were relayed by KIOD radio, the public, trained spotters and storm chasers. Hail sizes reported ranged from penny to half dollar.  At 1635 CST, a spotter reported nickel sized hail covering the road in Trenton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Modestly unstable air that was in place over eastern Nebraska during the afternoon and evening of May 5th helped fuel thunderstorm development from central into northeast Nebraska.  Also aiding thunderstorm development was a weak upper level disturbance that moved across the northern plains which pushed a weak surface trough into the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to ping pong ball size was reported in and around the Wisner area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of hot and very humid conditions was observed over eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa on June 22nd and 23rd.  High temperatures on the 22nd were in the lower to mid 90s and in the mid to upper 90s on the 23rd.  Overnight lows on the 23rd were in the mid to upper 70s.  Dew point temperatures on the 23rd were in the mid 70s to lower 80s.  The combination of the heat and humidity brought heat index values up into the 108 to 118 degree range during the afternoon of the 23rd.  Since these extremely uncomfortable temperatures occurred with light winds, generally less than 10 mph, conditions became deadly for livestock, especially during the afternoon of the 23rd.  It was estimated that at least 2,000 head of cattle died because of the heat in eastern Nebraska and western Iowa, most of them on the 23rd.  Conditions improved a bit during the late afternoon and early evening of the 23rd when isolated thunderstorms and associated outflow brought a little cooler temperatures and increased winds.

A cold front moved into southeast Nebraska and stalled during the early morning hours of June 6th.  Thunderstorms developed along the front in central and western Nebraska during the evening of the 5th and moved into eastern Nebraska early on the 6th.  The storms produced large hail and a few produced strong winds.  One storm that tracked across Saline county produced a prolonged period of hail that was up to half dollar-size which caused significant crop damage, estimated by emergency management to be over a million dollars.  The worst damage from that particular storm was along a 5 mile swath that stretched from Filmore county, across Saline county and then into extreme northern Gage county with an estimated 70,000 acres affected.  Although the corn crop had the most visible damage, the soybean and winter wheat crop suffered significant damage.

The damage was most significant along a 5 mile wide swath from around Tobias to Swanton to De Witt where the ground was covered white with hail.

Afternoon and early evening thunderstorms developed across portions of South Central Nebraska as an upper level disturbance moved through the region.  Thunderstorms developed mainly along a surface front which was slowly shifting south, and modest instability allowed for an isolated severe thunderstorm to develop near Chester in Thayer County, where pea to half dollar size hail was reported.

An upper level low pressure system moving across the Central Plains was the main forcing mechanism for thunderstorm development for portions of South Central Nebraska.  A few of these evening thunderstorms became severe, dropping penny to quarter size hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail reported near Interstate 80.

A weak cold front, possibly reinforced by thunderstorms that occurred either earlier in the afternoon or during the previous evening, caused a few severe thunderstorms to develop from southwest Iowa into southeast Nebraska.  Due to hot and humid conditions present over the region, the thunderstorms were mainly the wet micro-burst variety although some hail was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter estimated thunderstorm wind gusts of 60 mph lasted for around 20 minutes in the Du Bois area.  The winds were accompanied by torrential rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms that developed in western Nebraska during the late evening of June 22nd moved into western Iowa around noon on the 23rd.  The storms created an outflow boundary which collided with very unstable conditions, temperatures of 90 to 95 degrees and dew point temperatures in the upper 70s.  This caused new thunderstorm development near the Missouri River north of Omaha by mid afternoon.  These storms moved southeast clipping the Omaha metro area with and additional thunderstorm development farther west by evening.  The storms were mainly wet micro-burst producers although some hail was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A decaying thunderstorm flattened corn in the Tobias area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong early spring system impacted South Central Nebraska during the first weekend in April. A large upper level low pressure system moved out of the southern Rocky Mountains and crossed the Central Plains. A tight pressure gradient brought a windy start to the weekend, and along with increasing large scale ascent and expansion of precipitation across the area, a surface boundary helped to provide a focus for afternoon thunderstorm development.  The slow progress of this system had allowed for more moisture to work into the region, and with plenty of shear available, some thunderstorms became severe, dropping pea to half dollar size hail.  Two brief, weak tornadoes touched down in Clay County, causing some damage near Fairfield, where outbuildings were destroyed, a roof was torn off a barn, and two homes were damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense low pressure that tracked along the Kansas and Nebraska border and then across northern Missouri brought areas of heavy snow and blizzard conditions to sections of northeast Nebraska during the late afternoon and overnight hours of April 4th and 5th.  The snow was accompanied by north winds of 25 to 35 mph with gusts to 50 mph.  One area of heavy snow, along with blizzard conditions, developed during the evening of April 4th from near Albion north and northeast toward the South Dakota border.  A second area of a heavy wet snow developed later that evening and overnight from David City and Columbus northeast into western Iowa.  Since the snow across this area was wetter due to surface temperatures around 32 or 33 degrees...the poorer visibilities were mainly confined to when the heaviest snow was falling and blowing snow did not substantially reduce visibilities after the snow decreased.  Heavier snow totals from the storm included 10 inches in Tekamah, 9 inches in Neligh, 8 inches in David City and around 7 inches in Schuyler and Bloomfield.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow and north winds of 25 to 35 mph with gusts to 50 mph brought blizzard conditions, especially in open areas, to Cedar county from the evening hours of April 4th until the morning of the 5th.  Although the snow decreased or ended by late morning on the 5th, the strong winds persisted through the afternoon causing continued drifting snow. Snowfall over the county was generally between 4 and 7 inches, with 7 inches at Hartington the heaviest reported in Cedar county.  The snow and strong winds closed roads in the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled front extending from extreme southeast Nebraska into central Kansas, and an upper level low pressure trough to the west, brought scattered severe thunderstorms to eastern Nebraska during much of Sunday June 7th into the very early morning hours of June 8th.  Temperatures climbed into the lower 80s in far southeast Nebraska and southwest Iowa on the 7th, while readings remained in the 50s in northeast Nebraska.  Elevated storms, a few of which dropped large hail, moved across northeast Nebraska from late morning through the afternoon hours of the 7th.  By late afternoon of that day several supercell thunderstorms developed along the stalled front.  These storms shifted out of the region by early evening but additional thunderstorms that developed near the boundary in Kansas moved back into southeast Nebraska later that evening into the early overnight hours.  Although the environment was favorable for tornadoes this evening, in Nebraska the storms produced large hail and a few strong wind reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to half dollar-size was reported from the north part of Fairbury to 11 miles north of town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front that touched off isolated severe thunderstorms on April 24th and early on the 25th stalled over northeast Kansas and northwest Missouri on the 25th.  The front then returned north as a warm front during the morning of April 26th.  Temperatures south of the front climbed into the 70s with 40s and 50s prevailing to the north.  This contrast, combined with an upper level disturbance, helped kick off a line of thunderstorms north of the returning warm front.  The storms produced large hail as far north as the Omaha area and some damaging wind gusts in southwest Iowa east and southeast of Omaha.  In addition, with the storms tracking across the same area for many hours, some heavy rain fell over far southeast Nebraska and southwest Iowa.  Doppler Radar estimated and cooperative observers reported that some locations received 3 inches or more for 24 hour rainfall amounts from the storm.  However, no significant flooding was reported.

An intense upper low that tracked along the Kansas and Nebraska border and resultant surface low pressure system helped spawn a few severe thunderstorms, and even a tornado, over southeast Nebraska.  The storms developed near a warm front that extended from the low into southeast Nebraska.  Surface temperatures south of the warm front climbed into the 60s and 70s but dewpoint temperatures both ahead of and just behind the warm front managed to only climb into the 30s and 40s, somewhat limiting the coverage of the severe thunderstorms.  This system produced blizzard conditions and areas of heavy snow from the Nebraska Sandhills into northeast Nebraska during the afternoon and overnight hours of April 4th and 5th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado caused damage to a pole shed and roof of a house south of Pickerell.  Tree damage was also noted in this area.

A strong, slow moving upper level low pressure system shifted east out of the Rocky Mountains and onto the plains during the daytime hours of March 23rd.  A well vertically stacked system, it brought strong winds as well as thunderstorms to portions of South Central Nebraska.  With strong forcing and a tight pressure gradient across the area, this lead to widespread sustained wind speeds of 35 to 40 mph and gusts near 60 mph, which lingered into the evening hours.  These winds blew some dust around, causing some problems for travelers, also making it difficult to fight a number of wildfires which developed across the area.  Some strong to severe thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours, dropping penny to half dollar size hail across eastern portions of the area, with some damage reported in Hamilton and Fillmore Counties.

Daytime heating combined with moderate amounts of moisture and relatively cool air aloft near a northwest moving weak upper level low helped kick off afternoon thunderstorms across southeast Nebraska.  One storm produced a brief downburst causing damage near Auburn.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm downburst tore the roof off of a barn southeast of Auburn.  The winds were estimated at over 60 mph.

Intense low pressure that tracked along the Kansas and Nebraska border and then across northern Missouri brought areas of heavy snow and blizzard conditions to sections of northeast Nebraska during the late afternoon and overnight hours of April 4th and 5th.  The snow was accompanied by north winds of 25 to 35 mph with gusts to 50 mph.  One area of heavy snow, along with blizzard conditions, developed during the evening of April 4th from near Albion north and northeast toward the South Dakota border.  A second area of a heavy wet snow developed later that evening and overnight from David City and Columbus northeast into western Iowa.  Since the snow across this area was wetter due to surface temperatures around 32 or 33 degrees...the poorer visibilities were mainly confined to when the heaviest snow was falling and blowing snow did not substantially reduce visibilities after the snow decreased.  Heavier snow totals from the storm included 10 inches in Tekamah, 9 inches in Neligh, 8 inches in David City and around 7 inches in Schuyler and Bloomfield.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain changed to a brief period of freezing rain then to a heavy wet snow during the late evening hours of April 4th and continued into the morning of the 5th.  The precipitation was accompanied by north winds of 25 to 35 mph with gusts to 50 mph.  Although snowfall ended during the late morning of the 5th, the strong winds caused continued drifting snow in open areas into the afternoon hours.  The ice and heavy wet snow caused scattered power outages across the county.  Snowfall amounts were reported to have been around 4 to 6 inches across the county based on newspaper reports, amounts received from surrounding counties and visible satellite imagery taken the next day.  Travel was extremely difficult at times.

The ice and heavy wet snow and strong winds snapped power poles from around Scribner to Snyder and caused power outages in those areas north into Cuming county and southeast toward Hooper and also caused hazardous driving conditions. In some cases power was out until the evening of the 5th. Cell phone service was also interrupted due to a tower being blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot and unstable air, characterized by temperatures in the 90s and dew point temperatures in the 70s, stretched from eastern Kansas into south central Nebraska by mid afternoon on June 17th.  Northeast of that region, over most of eastern Nebraska, temperatures were also quite warm, in the upper 80s to lower 90s, but dew point temperatures were a bit lower, mostly in the 60s.  Supercell thunderstorms developed along this moisture discontinuity near the Kansas border during the early afternoon hours.  At least one of these storms produced a tornado.  Later in the afternoon and into the evening other storms that developed in central Nebraska on the nose of the warm and unstable air moved across the region producing mainly large hail but also areas of damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The first round of hail from a multi-updraft supercell thunderstorm that moved across the area this afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A bowing line of thunderstorms produced strong winds and large hail across southwest Nebraska during the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds and small hail were reported by a cooperative observer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms which developed in north central Nebraska during the late evening of the 17th tracked across northeast Nebraska during the early morning hours of the 18th.  The storms bowed out across Knox and Cedar counties producing damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail was accompanied by thunderstorm winds estimated at 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms moved northeast out of Colorado and into southwest Nebraska during the afternoon hours. The storms initially produced large hail.  However, then the storms started to develop into a bow echo as they moved northeast and produced very strong winds and a couple of brief tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado developed on the northwest corner of a bow echo and touched down very briefly.  It ripped a 70 pound cover off a hot tub and tossed it 150 feet into a field. It also tore off shingles from roofs.  The storm that produced the tornado continued northeast and produced another brief tornado.

Heavier snow totals from the storm included 10 inches in Tekamah, 9 inches in Neligh, 8 inches in David City and around 7 inches in Schuyler and Bloomfield.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain changed briefly to a period of freezing rain then to a heavy wet snow in the late evening of April 4th with snow continuing into the mid morning hours of the 5th. The precipitation was accompanied by north winds of 25 to 35 mph with gusts to 50 mph.  Although snowfall ended during the late morning of the 5th, the strong winds caused continued drifting snow in open areas into the afternoon hours.  Snowfall amounts were between 2 and 6 inches across the county based on reports from surrounding counties and satellite pictures taken the next day.  The ice and heavy wet snow and strong winds snapped power poles south of the county but some lines were downed causing power outages in the West Point, Wisner and Bancroft areas.  Travel was also very difficult.

A period of hot and very humid conditions was observed over eastern Nebraska and southwest Iowa on June 22nd and 23rd.  High temperatures on the 22nd were in the lower to mid 90s and in the mid to upper 90s on the 23rd.  Overnight lows on the 23rd were in the mid to upper 70s.  Dew point temperatures on the 23rd were in the mid 70s to lower 80s.  The combination of the heat and humidity brought heat index values up into the 108 to 118 degree range during the afternoon of the 23rd.  Since these extremely uncomfortable temperatures occurred with light winds, generally less than 10 mph, conditions became deadly for livestock, especially during the afternoon of the 23rd.  It was estimated that at least 2,000 head of cattle died because of the heat in eastern Nebraska and western Iowa, most of them on the 23rd.  Conditions improved a bit during the late afternoon and early evening of the 23rd when isolated thunderstorms and associated outflow brought a little cooler temperatures and increased winds.

A warm front that moved across eastern Nebraska during the afternoon and evening of May 31st was followed by a slow-moving cold front the next day.  The cold front caused a few severe thunderstorms to develop over southeast Nebraska, a few of which also produced very heavy rainfall.  Three to as much as six inches of rain was reported near the Kansas border from south of Beatrice to near Falls City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One inch hail fell for several minutes in the Verdon area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into southeast Nebraska and stalled during the early morning hours of June 6th.  Thunderstorms developed along the front in central and western Nebraska during the evening of the 5th and moved into eastern Nebraska early on the 6th.  The storms produced large hail and a few produced strong winds.  One storm that tracked across Saline county produced a prolonged period of hail that was up to half dollar-size which caused significant crop damage, estimated by emergency management to be over a million dollars.  The worst damage from that particular storm was along a 5 mile swath that stretched from Filmore county, across Saline county and then into extreme northern Gage county with an estimated 70,000 acres affected.  Although the corn crop had the most visible damage, the soybean and winter wheat crop suffered significant damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball-size hail was reported in Johnson by emergency management.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong early spring system impacted South Central Nebraska during the first weekend in April. A large upper level low pressure system moved out of the southern Rocky Mountains and crossed the Central Plains. A tight pressure gradient brought a windy start to the weekend, and along with increasing large scale ascent and expansion of precipitation across the area, a surface boundary helped to provide a focus for afternoon thunderstorm development.  The slow progress of this system had allowed for more moisture to work into the region, and with plenty of shear available, some thunderstorms became severe, dropping pea to half dollar size hail.  Two brief, weak tornadoes touched down in Clay County, causing some damage near Fairfield, where outbuildings were destroyed, a roof was torn off a barn, and two homes were damaged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail reported was mainly pea size, with a few quarter to half dollar size hailstones.

The cold front caused a few severe thunderstorms to develop over southeast Nebraska, a few of which also produced very heavy rainfall.  Three to as much as six inches of rain was reported near the Kansas border from south of Beatrice to near Falls City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain was reported across sections of Richardson county during the evening of June 1 with a little over 6 inches reported 6 miles south of Humboldt.  Law enforcement reported the rain caused minimal flooding or damage but 1 to 2 inches of water covered Highway 8 near mile marker 129, about 1/2 mile east of the Junction of Highways 105 and 8 for a while.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled front extending from extreme southeast Nebraska into central Kansas, and an upper level low pressure trough to the west, brought scattered severe thunderstorms to eastern Nebraska during much of Sunday June 7th into the very early morning hours of June 8th.  Temperatures climbed into the lower 80s in far southeast Nebraska and southwest Iowa on the 7th, while readings remained in the 50s in northeast Nebraska.  Elevated storms, a few of which dropped large hail, moved across northeast Nebraska from late morning through the afternoon hours of the 7th.  By late afternoon of that day several supercell thunderstorms developed along the stalled front.  These storms shifted out of the region by early evening but additional thunderstorms that developed near the boundary in Kansas moved back into southeast Nebraska later that evening into the early overnight hours.  Although the environment was favorable for tornadoes this evening, in Nebraska the storms produced large hail and a few strong wind reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter-size hail was observed a little south of De Witt.  An unsubstantiated report of 2.5 inch hail was also received in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms that developed west of the region moved into eastern Nebraska and western Iowa during the early morning hours of June 19th.  A fairly strong cap was overcome, aided by a low level jet and an upper level disturbance which provided some cooling aloft.  The strongest storms occurred in Butler county Nebraska as a cluster bowed out producing scattered wind damage.  Although the thunderstorms weakened somewhat, the storms continued to produce occasional wind damage as they progressed into western Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Nebraska Department of Roads sensor 3 miles south of David City measured a thunderstorm wind gust of 78 mph.  The strong winds caused scattered tree, fence and house damage in the David City area and surrounding communities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The same strong storm system which caused severe weather across portions of the region on April 4th, brought a round of winter weather to portions of South Central Nebraska during the overnight hours and into the first half of the day on April 5th.  This strong low pressure system tracked near the Nebraska and Kansas state line, with strong winds and snow on the northern edge.  The area which saw the most impact was along and north of Interstate 80, where peak winds of 45 to 55 mph were reported.  Such strong winds combined with the falling snow made travel very difficult to impossible at times, with visibilities reduces down to less than one quarter of a mile.  With the blowing and drifting of the snow, taking measurements after the storm was quite challenging, but many snowfall totals of 2 to 6 inches were reported.  In anticipation of the storm, many towns across the area declared snow emergencies.

Portions of South Central Nebraska saw another round of thunderstorms just prior to midnight, as yet another upper level disturbance moved across the region.  A few strong to severe thunderstorms developed, with nickel size hail reported in Nuckolls County, and penny to golf ball size hail reported in Thayer County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball size hail reported along Highway 4.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system moving across the Central Plains was the main forcing mechanism for thunderstorm development for portions of South Central Nebraska.  A few of these evening thunderstorms became severe, dropping penny to quarter size hail.

An upper level low pressure system moving across the Central Plains was the main forcing mechanism for thunderstorm development for portions of South Central Nebraska.  A few of these evening thunderstorms became severe, dropping penny to quarter size hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail reported covering the roads in Sutton.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail reported covering the ground in Shickley.

Although the thunderstorms weakened somewhat, the storms continued to produce occasional wind damage as they progressed into western Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at over 70 mph downed numerous large trees southeast of Brainard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A supercell dropped south out of South Dakota and into north central Nebraska late in the evening.  The supercell produced strong winds...large hail...and 2 brief tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down north of Newport and overturned 2 center pivot irrigation systems, moved a 5000 bushel gran bin approximately 75 yards, snapped a large electrical transmission line pole, snapped a smaller wooden power pole, broke off numerous tree limbs, damaged the roof of an outbuilding, and picked up and spun around an empty grain truck traveling on Highway 20.  The truck was heading west and was picked up and deposited in the south ditch.  A storm chaser on Highway 20 also witnessed the tornado.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local media weather equipment measured a thunderstorm wind gust of 58 mph in the Wahoo area.  The winds knocked down a few tree limbs in town but overall the damage was nothing major according to the county Emergency Manager.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms which developed in north central Nebraska during the late evening of the 17th tracked across northeast Nebraska during the early morning hours of the 18th.  The storms bowed out across Knox and Cedar counties producing damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at 60 mph knocked the power out in Orchard and flipped a center pivot 3 miles east of town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms moved across eastern Colorado into western Kansas and southwest Nebraska during the evening, producing heavy rainfall and a few reports of quarter size hail.

A 40 to 50 knot low level jet nosing into the region brought another round of thunderstorms to portions of south central Nebraska during the morning hours.  With modest instability available, some thunderstorms were strong to severe, dropping penny to ping pong ball size hail.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pea to quarter size hail reported lasting 15 minutes.

An upper level shortwave trough moving across the region helped to spark off a few late afternoon and evening thunderstorms across portions of south central Nebraska.  A few of these thunderstorms were able to drop pea to quarter size hail, along with some locally heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime to quarter size hail was reported along with heavy rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface cold front and upper level trough moving across the Plains brought scattered thunderstorms to south central Nebraska.  Though these thunderstorms occurred during the overnight hours, enough instability was available for a few strong to severe thunderstorms, with nickel to quarter size hail reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms that developed in western Nebraska during the late evening of June 22nd moved into western Iowa around noon on the 23rd.  The storms created an outflow boundary which collided with very unstable conditions, temperatures of 90 to 95 degrees and dew point temperatures in the upper 70s.  This caused new thunderstorm development near the Missouri River north of Omaha by mid afternoon.  These storms moved southeast clipping the Omaha metro area with and additional thunderstorm development farther west by evening.  The storms were mainly wet micro-burst producers although some hail was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A micro-burst thunderstorm produced wind gusts estimated at over 60 mph.  The winds knocked down trees and power lines in the southeast part of Omaha.  Up to 6800 OPPD customers lost their power from the storm.  The Omaha Public Works Department received about 15 reports of tree damage and a similar number of traffic light outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex which moved down the Missouri River during the predawn hours of July 8th spread an outflow boundary into southeast Nebraska. A few severe thunderstorms developed on this boundary during the mid to late morning hours before the complex moved into Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to quarter-size fell from just northwest of Tecumseh into the Tecumseh area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex which moved down the Missouri River during the predawn hours of July 8th spread an outflow boundary into southeast Nebraska. A few severe thunderstorms developed on this boundary during the mid to late morning hours before the complex moved into Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel to quarter-size hail was reported by a spotter 3 miles north of Unadilla.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front which moved into southeast Nebraska during the morning of June 6th stalled from southeast Nebraska into central Kansas during the afternoon and evening of that day.  Temperatures by late afternoon climbed into the upper 80s and lower 90s south of the front while readings northwest of the front were in the 50s and 60s. Thunderstorms developed near the boundary over Kansas early in the evening, and developed or spread into southeast Nebraska during the mid evening hours.  The storms produced mainly large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail just under baseball-size fell from just west of Pawnee City into town.  The hail fell for 5 to 10 minutes before it started raining as it gradually shrunk to golf ball-size and smaller.  The hail damaged some crops, vehicles and homes in the area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to quarter-size was driven by strong winds causing substantial damage from around Adams in Gage county to near Sterling in Johnson county.  The damage near Sterling was not as severe as around the Adams area, but there was still some home, auto and crop damage noted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm complex that developed over South Dakota during the evening of July 7th moved southeast along an instability gradient and down the Missouri River reaching the Omaha area before weakening.  The storms mainly produced strong winds but some hail was also reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at nearly 60 mph split a few large trees near Wakefield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front kicked off a few severe thunderstorms over eastern Nebraska during the mid evening hours of July 24th.  Initially warm mid level temperatures had hindered development, but they were overcome as the front encountered a bit better low level inflow and instability.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to golf ball-size fell from south of the Clarkson and Howells area to near Schuyler.  The hail caused sporadic damage to shingles and siding in the region and was accompanied by strong winds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail was accompanied by winds estimated at up to 50 mph.  Apparently winds were stronger and hail a bit larger north of town.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail covered the ground.

A strong and slow moving spring storm caused severe thunderstorms to occur across north central Nebraska. As colder was drawn into the system, strong winds and snow combined to produce blizzard conditions across the western half of north central Nebraska.  Snowfall amounts ranged from 3 to 8 inches with snow drifts ranging up to 5 feet across Sheridan county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms developed in an unstable atmosphere along and ahead of a cold front as it progressed southeastward across western and north central Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The cooperative observer measured the wind gusts on a Davis weather station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Supercells developed rapidly in the late afternoon and produced numerous funnels and large hail. A nearly stationary supercell near Sutherland produced one tornado which a storm spotter caught on video. The slow movement of the storms also produced torrential rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nearly stationary storms produced up to 5 inches of rain in Sutherland and flooded streets and buildings.  Approximately 20 to 30 homes had water in their basement.  About 2 inches of water rain into the Sutherland school music and weight rooms and into the Sutherland Care Center hallways. The volunteer fire department sandbagged around the school, the retirement home, and several residences in Sutherland.  Interstate 80 and Highway 30 between Sutherland and Hershey were closed for a short period of time because of the heavy rain and hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening of June 18th and early morning hours of June 19th, supercell storm developed over western Nebraska and produced strong winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail covered the ground.

A warm front that moved across eastern Nebraska during the afternoon and evening of May 31st was followed by a slow-moving cold front the next day.  The cold front caused a few severe thunderstorms to develop over southeast Nebraska, a few of which also produced very heavy rainfall.  Three to as much as six inches of rain was reported near the Kansas border from south of Beatrice to near Falls City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The cooperative observer near Plymouth measured a thunderstorm wind gust of 59 mph.  The winds were accompanied by hail up to 1 inch in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved into southeast Nebraska and stalled during the early morning hours of June 6th.  Thunderstorms developed along the front in central and western Nebraska during the evening of the 5th and moved into eastern Nebraska early on the 6th.  The storms produced large hail and a few produced strong winds.  One storm that tracked across Saline county produced a prolonged period of hail that was up to half dollar-size which caused significant crop damage, estimated by emergency management to be over a million dollars.  The worst damage from that particular storm was along a 5 mile swath that stretched from Filmore county, across Saline county and then into extreme northern Gage county with an estimated 70,000 acres affected.  Although the corn crop had the most visible damage, the soybean and winter wheat crop suffered significant damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail at least up to quarter-size fell across the northern part of Gage county near the Pickrell area.  The hail caused considerable crop damage in northern Gage county and was a continuation of the storm that caused over a million dollars of crop damage in Saline county.  In Gage county crop damage alone was estimated at $90,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another round of afternoon thunderstorms developed across portions of South Central Nebraska along a weak surface boundary draped through the area.  These thunderstorms continued on into the evening, with some becoming strong to severe, and while only dime to nickel size hail fell, wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated at 60 to 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another round of afternoon thunderstorms developed across portions of South Central Nebraska along a weak surface boundary draped through the area.  These thunderstorms continued on into the evening, with some becoming strong to severe, and while only dime to nickel size hail fell, wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusts estimated at 60 mph were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early on in the afternoon, a few scattered thunderstorms developed across eastern portions of South Central Nebraska, with one severe thunderstorm across Thayer County, where quarter to baseball size hail was reported.  The main show came later during the early evening hours, after a surface warm front had moved north into the area.  Near the front, good instability and shear was present, as was moisture.  Scattered thunderstorms developed along the edge of the mid level cap near the surface front, and with the environment present, some became strong to severe.  The main storm of the day developed in Buffalo County, then moved east across Hall and Hamilton Counties.  In each of these counties, this storm produced a tornado, with the largest occurring in Hamilton County, along Highway 34 near the town of Aurora.  This tornado caused EF2 damage to a home in its path, before dissipating a few miles west of Aurora.  Other damage caused by this storm included downed power poles and derailed train cars, as well as roof damage to the Iams plant caused by strong inflow winds.  Weaker tornadoes were reported on the south side of Grand Island in Hall County, as well as north of Gibbon in Buffalo County, where EF1 damage occurred at a few farmsteads.  Penny to tennis ball size hail was also reported across the area, causing damage to crops, with one report of broken house windows in Valley County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A third tornado touched down north of Gibbon, with no significant damage reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot and unstable air, characterized by temperatures in the 90s and dew point temperatures in the 70s, stretched from eastern Kansas into south central Nebraska by mid afternoon on June 17th.  Northeast of that region, over most of eastern Nebraska, temperatures were also quite warm, in the upper 80s to lower 90s, but dew point temperatures were a bit lower, mostly in the 60s.  Supercell thunderstorms developed along this moisture discontinuity near the Kansas border during the early afternoon hours.  At least one of these storms produced a tornado.  Later in the afternoon and into the evening other storms that developed in central Nebraska on the nose of the warm and unstable air moved across the region producing mainly large hail but also areas of damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to golf ball-size was reported from southwest of Falls City into town.  Many vehicles in town sustained hail damage, some substantially, along with roofs and sidings of homes.  The hail was from a multi-updraft supercell thunderstorm that moved across the area that afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms which developed in north central Nebraska during the late evening of the 17th tracked across northeast Nebraska during the early morning hours of the 18th.  The storms bowed out across Knox and Cedar counties producing damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to baseball-size was reported during the storm which also produced wind gusts |estimated at 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms which developed in north central Nebraska during the late evening of the 17th tracked across northeast Nebraska during the early morning hours of the 18th.  The storms bowed out across Knox and Cedar counties producing damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated at close to 70 mph caused fairly widespread tree damage in the Bloomfield area.  Large trees and branches fell on a few homes and vehicles causing damage and crops and irrigation systems in the region also sustained damage.  This storm also produced hail up to baseball-size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms that developed west of the region moved into eastern Nebraska and western Iowa during the early morning hours of June 19th.  A fairly strong cap was overcome, aided by a low level jet and an upper level disturbance which provided some cooling aloft.  The strongest storms occurred in Butler county Nebraska as a cluster bowed out producing scattered wind damage.  Although the thunderstorms weakened somewhat, the storms continued to produce occasional wind damage as they progressed into western Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A local mesonet observation near the Washington county/Douglas county line south of Blair measured a thunderstorm wind gust of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface cold front slowly moving across the area brought another round of late evening and overnight thunderstorms to portions of South Central Nebraska, aided by an increased low level jet.  Decent shear and instability present allowed for thunderstorms to become strong to severe, dropping penny to golf ball size hail as well as producing 60 to 70 mph winds.  Crop damage was reported along Highway 6.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours across northern and eastern portions of South Central Nebraska along weak surface boundaries left over from previous convection.  Some thunderstorms became strong to severe, dropping nickel to quarter size hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pea to nickel size hail was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another round of afternoon thunderstorms developed across portions of South Central Nebraska along a weak surface boundary draped through the area.  These thunderstorms continued on into the evening, with some becoming strong to severe, and while only dime to nickel size hail fell, wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds estimated at 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along and north of a warm front that extended across northern Kansas during the mid evening hours of June 15th.  The storms produced very heavy rain, and some locations picked up 2 inches or so in an hour or two, which produced localized flash flooding.  An isolated storm also produce large hail in Seward county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain, for the second night in a row, caused flash flooding along Big Muddy Creek north of Falls City.  The flooding closed Highway 73 for an hour or so.  Damage along Highway 73 was minimal.

A stalled surface boundary draped through the region, aided later by an increasing low level jet, provided a focus for the development of thunderstorms across South Central Nebraska.  Warmer air aloft kept southern areas capped, with activity generally confined to areas along and north of Highway 6, and west of Highway 183.  Decent shear and instability allowed for thunderstorms to become strong to severe, and along with numerous reports of hail to the size of quarters and larger, wind gusts of 65 to 75 mph were also reported.  The hardest hit area was across eastern Buffalo County and western Hall County.  Along with thousands of acres of crops being damaged or destroyed in this area, the town of Shelton was also affected by the strongest winds.  Damage reports included a metal building and billboard being destroyed, as well as trees and power lines knocked down.  There were many other reports across the area of damage to homes and vehicles caused by the large hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level ridge anchored across the central U.S. allowed for hot air to build into the region, and along with surface dewpoints in the upper 60s and lower 70s, made for muggy conditions.  Heat indices across the area reached into the 100 to 110 degree range on the 23rd, which lead to the deaths of approximately 4000 head of cattle.  The afternoon high temperatures reached into the mid to upper 90s, which was a change from the previous few weeks, where highs had generally been in the 70s and 80s.  The cooler than normal high temperatures had prevented the cattle from properly shedding their winter coats, which aided in their overheating.

Early on in the afternoon, a few scattered thunderstorms developed across eastern portions of South Central Nebraska, with one severe thunderstorm across Thayer County, where quarter to baseball size hail was reported.  The main show came later during the early evening hours, after a surface warm front had moved north into the area.  Near the front, good instability and shear was present, as was moisture.  Scattered thunderstorms developed along the edge of the mid level cap near the surface front, and with the environment present, some became strong to severe.  The main storm of the day developed in Buffalo County, then moved east across Hall and Hamilton Counties.  In each of these counties, this storm produced a tornado, with the largest occurring in Hamilton County, along Highway 34 near the town of Aurora.  This tornado caused EF2 damage to a home in its path, before dissipating a few miles west of Aurora.  Other damage caused by this storm included downed power poles and derailed train cars, as well as roof damage to the Iams plant caused by strong inflow winds.  Weaker tornadoes were reported on the south side of Grand Island in Hall County, as well as north of Gibbon in Buffalo County, where EF1 damage occurred at a few farmsteads.  Penny to tennis ball size hail was also reported across the area, causing damage to crops, with one report of broken house windows in Valley County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter to ping pong ball size hail was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled surface boundary draped through the region, aided later by an increasing low level jet, provided a focus for the development of thunderstorms across South Central Nebraska. There were many other reports across the area of damage to homes and vehicles caused by the large hail.

A strong upper level ridge anchored across the central U.S. allowed for hot air to build into the region, and along with surface dewpoints in the upper 60s and lower 70s, made for muggy conditions.  Heat indices across the area reached into the 100 to 110 degree range on the 23rd, which lead to the deaths of approximately 4000 head of cattle.  The afternoon high temperatures reached into the mid to upper 90s, which was a change from the previous few weeks, where highs had generally been in the 70s and 80s.  The cooler than normal high temperatures had prevented the cattle from properly shedding their winter coats, which aided in their overheating.

A surface warm front draped across the region was the focus for afternoon thunderstorm development as an upper level disturbance moved through.  Breaks in the clouds during the afternoon allowed for instability to quickly increase, and along with the already present deep layer shear, some thunderstorms became severe, dropping hail from the size of nickels up to tennis balls.  As the low level jet began to increase during the evening, this enhanced low level shear, aiding in the development of a brief, weak tornado in Adams County near the town of Assumption.  This EF0 tornado overturned a couple of pivots, and caused some damage to crops and to one farmstead, where minor tree and outbuilding damage was reported.  Another tornado touchdown was reported south of Shelton, but was very brief and caused no damage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel to golf ball size hail was reported.
