A weak upper level disturbance crossing the region provided enough lift for scattered thunderstorms to develop across portions of South Central Nebraska.  With instability and shear on the low side, there were no reports of hail, but there was plenty of moisture available, allowing for locally heavy rainfall.  Furnas County felt the most impact, where rainfall totals near 5 inches was reported, causing areas of flash flooding.  Water began to run across roads near Oxford, and Highway 136 was closed for a period of time.  Equipment had to eventually be called in to clear debris from the highway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm complex that developed north of low pressure in north central Kansas merged with thunderstorms that developed north of a warm front that was along the Kansas and Nebraska border.  With temperatures in the 70s and dew point temperatures around 70 near the front and low, instability was moderately high.  Initially the thunderstorms that developed north of the warm front produced a few large hail reports.  However, as the storms to the west merged with the activity farther east the main severe weather reports consisted of strong winds, especially from one cluster of storms that rolled from Saline and Gage county east into the southeast corner of Nebraska.  Around 12,000 customers lost their power from the storm, mostly from Lincoln and locations southwest through southeast of town.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An isolated report of 4 inches of rain was also received in this area.

Around 12,000 customers lost their power from the storm, mostly from Lincoln and locations southwest through southeast of town.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated at between 60 and 70 mph downed a few large trees which covered Highway 77 in Cortland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface cold front draped across the region, aided by an upper level disturbance crossing overhead, provided a focus for afternoon and evening thunderstorms development across portions of South Central Nebraska.  Available shear and instability allowed for some thunderstorms to become strong, with penny to nickel size hail reported, causing crop damage across Fillmore and Thayer Counties.

A stalled surface boundary draped through the region, aided later by an increasing low level jet, provided a focus for the development of thunderstorms across South Central Nebraska.  Warmer air aloft kept southern areas capped, with activity generally confined to areas along and north of Highway 6, and west of Highway 183.  Decent shear and instability allowed for thunderstorms to become strong to severe, and along with numerous reports of hail to the size of quarters and larger, wind gusts of 65 to 75 mph were also reported.  The hardest hit area was across eastern Buffalo County and western Hall County.  Along with thousands of acres of crops being damaged or destroyed in this area, the town of Shelton was also affected by the strongest winds.  Damage reports included a metal building and billboard being destroyed, as well as trees and power lines knocked down.  There were many other reports across the area of damage to homes and vehicles caused by the large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny to nickel size hail was reported.

A narrow line of severe thunderstorms developed along a cold front that moved into northeast Nebraska during the late afternoon of August 19th.  Meanwhile isolated supercell thunderstorms developed over southeast Nebraska into southwest Iowa due to an upper level disturbance that was tied into a much larger disturbance that helped push the cold front through the region.  The line of thunderstorms in northeast Nebraska weakened as it moved east of Columbus, but the storms southeast of there congealed into small line segments and were finally shoved east of the region as the front pushed through later in the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter to golf ball-size hail was reported from a few miles south of Wilber into town.  The hail broke some windows on vehicles in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front kicked off a few severe thunderstorms over eastern Nebraska during the mid evening hours of July 24th.  Initially warm mid level temperatures had hindered development, but they were overcome as the front encountered a bit better low level inflow and instability.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated at over 60 mph caused tree and scattered house damage from around 7 miles north of Schuyler into Schuyler.  Seven miles north of town the winds blew a kitchen window out of a house, and trees or large branches were downed in the Schuyler area causing scattered house damage.  The winds were accompanied by hail up to golf ball-size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early on in the afternoon, a few scattered thunderstorms developed across eastern portions of South Central Nebraska, with one severe thunderstorm across Thayer County, where quarter to baseball size hail was reported.  The main show came later during the early evening hours, after a surface warm front had moved north into the area.  Near the front, good instability and shear was present, as was moisture.  Scattered thunderstorms developed along the edge of the mid level cap near the surface front, and with the environment present, some became strong to severe.  The main storm of the day developed in Buffalo County, then moved east across Hall and Hamilton Counties.  In each of these counties, this storm produced a tornado, with the largest occurring in Hamilton County, along Highway 34 near the town of Aurora.  This tornado caused EF2 damage to a home in its path, before dissipating a few miles west of Aurora.  Other damage caused by this storm included downed power poles and derailed train cars, as well as roof damage to the Iams plant caused by strong inflow winds.  Weaker tornadoes were reported on the south side of Grand Island in Hall County, as well as north of Gibbon in Buffalo County, where EF1 damage occurred at a few farmsteads.  Penny to tennis ball size hail was also reported across the area, causing damage to crops, with one report of broken house windows in Valley County.

A powerful spring storm system tracked northeast from southern Colorado into north central Kansas and eastern Nebraska on April 4th and 5th, 2009. Widespread snow occurred as north winds increased to 30 to 40 mph with gusts over 50 mph at times. Blizzard conditions developed by early afternoon on April 4th and persisted into the early morning hours on April 5th. Snowfall amounts ranged from 6 to 18 inches across north central Nebraska with snowdrifts ranging from 3 to 7 feet. Snowfall of 3 to 6 inches occurred to the south across west central and southwest Nebraska with blizzard conditions. Road closures and power outages were reported in many areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions began across Arthur County by 11 am MST on April 4th.  Snowfall amounts of 8 to 12 inches with strong north winds of 30 to 50 mph caused snow drifts of 3 to 6 feet and near zero visibility.  Several calves died in the storm.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions began across Deuel County by 11 am MST on April 4, 2009.  Although snowfall amounts only ranged from 3 to 6 inches, strong north winds of 30 to 40 mph caused considerable blowing and drifting snow and reduced visibility to less than a quarter mile during the afternoon and evening hours. This caused the closure of Interstate 80 west of Big Spring at 1121 am MST, which continued through the afternoon and early evening hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms, a few severe, developed along a cold front that pushed slowly into southeast Nebraska during the mid to late afternoon hours of August 9th.  Temperatures ahead of the front climbed into the 90s with dew points in the 70s providing a modestly unstable environment.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm gust front, which preceded the rain from the storm, brought winds estimated at over 60 mph.  These strong winds continued until well after the rain had started which, according to local newspaper reports, lasted for at least 15 minutes.  The winds uprooted several big trees and snapped trees and limbs all over town.  The downed trees and limbs caused house and vehicle damage, blocked streets and downed power lines.  The downed power lines caused an hour and half power outage in Auburn and a 4 to 5 hour loss for rural areas surrounding town. A few transformers in the Auburn area were also damaged and a trailer was blown off of Highway 75 four miles south of town.  This was the second wind storm within a week to hit the Auburn area, the other was on Aug. 4th, although this one was deemed much worse by local emergency management.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm complex that developed north of low pressure in north central Kansas merged with thunderstorms that developed north of a warm front that was along the Kansas and Nebraska border.  With temperatures in the 70s and dew point temperatures around 70 near the front and low, instability was moderately high.  Initially the thunderstorms that developed north of the warm front produced a few large hail reports.  However, as the storms to the west merged with the activity farther east the main severe weather reports consisted of strong winds, especially from one cluster of storms that rolled from Saline and Gage county east into the southeast corner of Nebraska.  Around 12,000 customers lost their power from the storm, mostly from Lincoln and locations southwest through southeast of town.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated at 65 to 70 mph caused sporadic power outages near Wilber when the power pole a transformer was on snapped.  Several large limbs were also downed in town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually strong early season cold front brought snow to extreme southeast Wyoming and the southern Nebraska panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported 8 inches of snow six miles south of Harrisburg. Snow water equivalent was 0.66 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved southeast and became the focus for severe thunderstorm development into the evening hours across western and north central Nebraska. The storms initiated along the cold front and produced large hail and damaging winds. Heavy rainfall associated with the storms produced localized flash flooding in portions of Holt County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bessy RAWS site recorded a 61 mph wind gust.

Storms developed with the peak heating of the day across western Nebraska and produced large hail.

Moist and unstable conditions that prevailed over eastern Nebraska during the afternoon and evening hours of August 15th helped set the stage for slow moving thunderstorms that repeatedly developed and tracked across parts of northeast Nebraska.  The storms were aided by an unseasonably strong upper level system that was tracking along the Montana/Canadian border.  The storms developed in a broken west to east line generally to the northwest through north of Columbus.  The storms were eventually overtaken around midnight by a larger and generally north to south line of thunderstorms that had developed earlier that day over the western high plains.  The storms in northeast Nebraska were mainly heavy rain producers, but a few possessed strong rotation and brought some hail and gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Another round of heavy rain producing thunderstorms caused additional flash flooding across a few county roads from near Clearwater to around Brunswick.  Earlier in the evening over 3 inches of rain was reported around Clearwater and an additional inch or two of rain likely fell across the area either causing additional flooding or prolonging flooding that had already been occurring.   The flooding caused some crop and culvert damage and also washed gravel off of sections of county roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moist and unstable conditions that prevailed over eastern Nebraska during the afternoon and evening hours of August 15th helped set the stage for slow moving thunderstorms that repeatedly developed and tracked across parts of northeast Nebraska.  The storms were aided by an unseasonably strong upper level system that was tracking along the Montana/Canadian border.  The storms developed in a broken west to east line generally to the northwest through north of Columbus.  The storms were eventually overtaken around midnight by a larger and generally north to south line of thunderstorms that had developed earlier that day over the western high plains.  The storms in northeast Nebraska were mainly heavy rain producers, but a few possessed strong rotation and brought some hail and gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain, the heaviest of which fell west through southwest of Norfolk, caused flash flooding across a few county roads from near Battle Creek to south of Norfolk.  The water also covered one lane of Highway 81 south of Norfolk.  Damage was minimal and mainly consisted of some crop damage in low areas and some gravel washed off of county roads.  Rainfall was estimated to have at least been 2 to 4 inches over the much of the area based on surrounding reports and radar estimations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed northwest of a stationary front that extended across southeast Nebraska.  The storms were aided by an upper level disturbance that lifted northeast across South Dakota, a modest low level jet and relatively deep low level moisture over eastern Nebraska.  Although the storms dropped some large hail, they mostly produced strong and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail was accompanied by thunderstorm winds that downed some large tree limbs in the area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at around 60 mph downed a small tree northwest of Blair.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed northwest of a stationary front that extended across southeast Nebraska.  The storms were aided by an upper level disturbance that lifted northeast across South Dakota, a modest low level jet and relatively deep low level moisture over eastern Nebraska.  Although the storms dropped some large hail, they mostly produced strong and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated at around 60 mph downed 4 inch diameter tree limbs north of Nickerson.  The winds were accompanied by golf ball-size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moist and unstable conditions that prevailed over eastern Nebraska during the afternoon and evening hours of August 15th helped set the stage for slow moving thunderstorms that repeatedly developed and tracked across parts of northeast Nebraska.  The storms were aided by an unseasonably strong upper level system that was tracking along the Montana/Canadian border.  The storms developed in a broken west to east line generally to the northwest through north of Columbus.  The storms were eventually overtaken around midnight by a larger and generally north to south line of thunderstorms that had developed earlier that day over the western high plains.  The storms in northeast Nebraska were mainly heavy rain producers, but a few possessed strong rotation and brought some hail and gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused ditches and culverts to run full or flood across a few county roads from near Clearwater to around Brunswick.  A trained weather spotter near Clearwater measured 3.25 inches of rain in a several hour period through 745 pm CDT and additional heavy rain fell after that time in the area.  Besides some crop damage in low areas, gravel was washed off a few county roads and a couple of culverts sustained damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms developed with the peak heating of the day across western Nebraska and produced large hail.
Storms developed with the peak heating of the day across western Nebraska and produced large hail.

Scattered thunderstorms, a few severe, developed along a cold front that pushed slowly into southeast Nebraska during the mid to late afternoon hours of August 9th.  Temperatures ahead of the front climbed into the 90s with dew points in the 70s providing a modestly unstable environment.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated at over 60 mph downed large tree limbs in the area which caused some house and outbuilding damage.  Crops were also flattened around town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled surface boundary draped through the region, aided later by an increasing low level jet, provided a focus for the development of thunderstorms across South Central Nebraska.  Warmer air aloft kept southern areas capped, with activity generally confined to areas along and north of Highway 6, and west of Highway 183.  Decent shear and instability allowed for thunderstorms to become strong to severe, and along with numerous reports of hail to the size of quarters and larger, wind gusts of 65 to 75 mph were also reported.  The hardest hit area was across eastern Buffalo County and western Hall County.  Along with thousands of acres of crops being damaged or destroyed in this area, the town of Shelton was also affected by the strongest winds.  Damage reports included a metal building and billboard being destroyed, as well as trees and power lines knocked down.  There were many other reports across the area of damage to homes and vehicles caused by the large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated at 50 mph were also reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at over 60 mph split or downed a significant number of large trees in the Humboldt area.  The fallen trees caused scattered structural damage in the area, especially at the local cemetery where 20 to 30 trees were lost.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm complex that developed north of low pressure in north central Kansas merged with thunderstorms that developed north of a warm front that was along the Kansas and Nebraska border.  With temperatures in the 70s and dew point temperatures around 70 near the front and low, instability was moderately high.  Initially the thunderstorms that developed north of the warm front produced a few large hail reports.  However, as the storms to the west merged with the activity farther east the main severe weather reports consisted of strong winds, especially from one cluster of storms that rolled from Saline and Gage county east into the southeast corner of Nebraska.  Around 12,000 customers lost their power from the storm, mostly from Lincoln and locations southwest through southeast of town.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at over 60 mph caused heavy tree damage across the southern sections of Lincoln, mostly south of O Street, according to a Lincoln Electric System spokesperson.  Around 6,000 customers lost power.  Some hail as large as 2 inches in diameter fell from the storm across the southern sections of town also.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across south central Nebraska during the early morning hours, with an isolated strong thunderstorm in Dawson County, dropping penny size hail near Gothenburg.

An upper level disturbance helped to push a surface cold front south through the region, aiding in the development of evening thunderstorms across south central Nebraska.  Some thunderstorms became severe, dropping hail up to the size of gold balls.  A number of funnel clouds were reported with a storm near Cairo, which then shifted south into Grand Island, where additional funnel clouds were reported.

A potent upper level low pressure system brought heavy snow to much of western and north central Nebraska beginning on October 28th and persisting through October 30th.  Snowfall amounts of 6 to 12 inches fell across the eastern Panhandle on October 28th and 29th. As the upper level low moved from western Kansas into central Nebraska on October 29th and 30th, a swath of very heavy snow fell from southwestern Nebraska into portions of central and north central Nebraska.  Snowfall amounts in these areas ranged from 6 to 15 inches. Gusty north winds of 20 to 35 mph during the early morning hours on October 30th caused blowing and drifting snow with drifts from 3 to 4 feet in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to occasional heavy snowfall occurred across Arthur County during the evening hours on October 29th and persisted through the early morning hours of October 30th. Storm total snowfall from 5 to 7 inches occurred across Arthur County.  Gusty north winds caused blowing and drifting snow with drifts from 1 to 3 feet high.

Strong to severe thunderstorms developed during the evening hours as an upper level disturbance pushed a surface cold front across south central Nebraska.  With ample instability and shear for these thunderstorms to work with, hail up to the size of ping pong balls and winds of 60 mph were reported.

A weak upper level wave moving across the plains was enough to spark the development of scattered thunderstorms across portions of south central Nebraska.  Modest instability and shear allowed for some thunderstorms to become strong to severe. The most impact was felt across portions of Buffalo, Kearney, and Phelps Counties, where 60 to 80 mph winds and hail to the size of quarters were reported.  Numerous reports of damage came in from the Odessa area, where pivots were overturned, vehicles were blown off of the interstate, and the roof and garage of a home sustained damage.

A strong supercell developed over Hooker County during the late afternoon hours and moved southward.  Strong rotation and large hail was reported with the storm.  Because the storm passed over a very rural area, a tornado could never be confirmed.  Another supercell which produced large hail developed during the evening hours across north central Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported tennis ball size hail.  The hail damaged vehicles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the second time in a few days thunderstorms which developed over South Dakota turned southeast along an instability axis and on the edge of a warm mid-level cap.  These storms dropped large hail, very heavy rain and gusty winds as they moved along the Missouri River.  This time the storms weakened well north of the Omaha area.  However, before they weakened...the storms that tracked across northern Cedar caused extensive crop damage along with flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced wind gusts estimated at nearly 70 mph north of Bancroft before the storm weakened.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm complex that developed north of low pressure in north central Kansas merged with thunderstorms that developed north of a warm front that was along the Kansas and Nebraska border.  With temperatures in the 70s and dew point temperatures around 70 near the front and low, instability was moderately high.  Initially the thunderstorms that developed north of the warm front produced a few large hail reports.  However, as the storms to the west merged with the activity farther east the main severe weather reports consisted of strong winds, especially from one cluster of storms that rolled from Saline and Gage county east into the southeast corner of Nebraska.  Around 12,000 customers lost their power from the storm, mostly from Lincoln and locations southwest through southeast of town.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated at least at 65 to 70 mph knocked down trees and power lines, flipped some center pivots and flattened corn from south of Goehner into the Milford area.  An on-site weather station near Goehner measured 65 mph wind gusts with this storm, but more damage and probably a little stronger winds occurred in the Milford area.  Vehicles and homes sustained damage due to the fallen trees, and several roads, including Highway 6, were temporarily closed because of downed trees.  Milford city maintenance workers estimated it would take approximately two weeks to clean up.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the second time in a few days thunderstorms which developed over South Dakota turned southeast along an instability axis and on the edge of a warm mid-level cap.  These storms dropped large hail, very heavy rain and gusty winds as they moved along the Missouri River.  This time the storms weakened well north of the Omaha area.  However, before they weakened...the storms that tracked across northern Cedar caused extensive crop damage along with flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to golf ball-size was driven by winds at least at 50 mph causing extensive crop damage in the Bow Valley area.  The damage was from the same storm that caused a swath of damage from near St. Helena and Wynot east to the Dixon county line.

A potent upper level low pressure system brought heavy snow to much of western and north central Nebraska beginning on October 28th and persisting through October 30th.  Snowfall amounts of 6 to 12 inches fell across the eastern Panhandle on October 28th and 29th. As the upper level low moved from western Kansas into central Nebraska on October 29th and 30th, a swath of very heavy snow fell from southwestern Nebraska into portions of central and north central Nebraska.  Snowfall amounts in these areas ranged from 6 to 15 inches. Gusty north winds of 20 to 35 mph during the early morning hours on October 30th caused blowing and drifting snow with drifts from 3 to 4 feet in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to occasional heavy snowfall occurred across Grant County during the overnight hours on October 29th and persisted through the early morning hours of October 30th. Storm total snowfall from 4 to 7 inches occurred across Grant County.  Gusty north winds caused blowing and drifting snow with drifts from 1 to 3 feet high.

The heaviest snowfall amounts occurred across the northern half of Lincoln County into the southern half of Custer County where areas received from 12 to as much as 17 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large and relatively slow moving storm brought a prolonged winter storm and even, for a time, blizzard conditions to eastern Nebraska and western Iowa from late on Dec. 7th through the early morning hours of Dec. 9th.  The storm was driven by an upper level disturbance that tracked from the west coast across the Rockies and deepened over the Mississippi River Valley.  The heaviest snow fell in advance of when the stronger winds arrived, mainly during the morning and afternoon of Dec. 8th.  However, as north winds increased to 30 to 50 mph during the night of the 8th and early on the 9th, visibilities frequently dropped to near zero, especially in open areas.  Considerable drifting snow also occurred, in many cases closing roads back shut almost as fast as they could be opened.  This prompted many counties to pull snow plows off the roads for a while during the night of the 8th and early on the 9th.  Many schools were closed for 3 days because of the storm; due the forecast of heavy snow to begin on the 7th, because of poor visibilities and roads that were drifted shut on the 8th and still some closed roads and bitter cold wind chills which followed the storm on the 9th.  An elderly Omaha man was found dead during the evening of the 8th when he apparently had car trouble and returned to his apartment and was found dead sitting down in a chair outside. ||Total snowfall from the storm was 6 to 15 inches over most of eastern Nebraska and western Iowa.  Higher amounts in eastern Nebraska included 15 inches in Union, 14 inches in Columbus, around 12 inches at the NWS in Valley, at Tekamah, Uehling, Steele City and Weeping Water.  In western Iowa heavier amounts included 12 inches in Clarinda and Hastings and around 11 inches in Harlan, Red Oak and Little Sioux.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 8 to 12 inches fell across much of the county from this storm with around 10 inches measured at Ft. Calhoun. The snow began during the late evening of 12/7 and continued into the early morning hours of 12/9.  Winds were not that strong when the snow started, but north winds increased during the afternoon of 12/8 and especially that evening into the next morning.  Frequent wind gusts of 40 mph or more were observed with a peak gust of 51 mph measured by the AWOS at Blair.  The winds produced considerable blowing and drifting snow with blizzard conditions during the evening and early morning hours of the 8th and 9th. Some county roads were drifted shut.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level storm system pushed southeast out of Canada and through the Northern and Central Plains, accompanied by a strong surface cold front passage.  Behind this front, a stout band of snow developed across portions of south central Nebraska, driven by an area of increased middle level frontogenetical forcing.  This snow initially developed during the afternoon and early evening hours on the 9th in northwest Nebraska, before spreading into south central Nebraska during the late evening and overnight hours, and tapered off to an end by noon on the 10th.  Accumulating snowfall was confined to areas generally along and north of Highway 6, with the heaviest amounts along and north of Highway 92 and west of Highway 281.  The highest totals ranged from 6 to 14 inches, with the heaviest snow falling in a narrow band across Sherman, Valley, and Howard Counties.  Further east across Nance, Merrick, and Polk Counties, snow accumulations were generally in the 5 to 8 inch range, and areas south of Highway 92 saw anywhere from a trace to 4 inches of snow. The primary impact from this event was that farmers were once again delayed from harvesting crops in what had already been a late harvest season for many areas.

A large and relatively slow moving storm brought a prolonged winter storm and even, for a time, blizzard conditions to eastern Nebraska and western Iowa from late on Dec. 7th through the early morning hours of Dec. 9th.  The storm was driven by an upper level disturbance that tracked from the west coast across the Rockies and deepened over the Mississippi River Valley.  The heaviest snow fell in advance of when the stronger winds arrived, mainly during the morning and afternoon of Dec. 8th.  However, as north winds increased to 30 to 50 mph during the night of the 8th and early on the 9th, visibilities frequently dropped to near zero, especially in open areas.  Considerable drifting snow also occurred, in many cases closing roads back shut almost as fast as they could be opened.  This prompted many counties to pull snow plows off the roads for a while during the night of the 8th and early on the 9th.  Many schools were closed for 3 days because of the storm; due the forecast of heavy snow to begin on the 7th, because of poor visibilities and roads that were drifted shut on the 8th and still some closed roads and bitter cold wind chills which followed the storm on the 9th.  An elderly Omaha man was found dead during the evening of the 8th when he apparently had car trouble and returned to his apartment and was found dead sitting down in a chair outside. ||Total snowfall from the storm was 6 to 15 inches over most of eastern Nebraska and western Iowa.  Higher amounts in eastern Nebraska included 15 inches in Union, 14 inches in Columbus, around 12 inches at the NWS in Valley, at Tekamah, Uehling, Steele City and Weeping Water.  In western Iowa heavier amounts included 12 inches in Clarinda and Hastings and around 11 inches in Harlan, Red Oak and Little Sioux.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 8 to 10 inches fell across much of the county with 10 inches reported in Verdon. The snow began during the evening of 12/7 and continued into the early morning hours of 12/9.  Winds were not that strong when the snow started, but north winds increased during the afternoon of 12/8 and especially that evening into the next morning.  Frequent gusts around 40 mph were observed and a peak wind of 44 mph was measured at Falls City. The winds produced considerable blowing and drifting snow with blizzard conditions during the evening and early morning hours of the 8th and 9th.

A large upper level low pressure system over the northern plains combined with an unusually cold air mass that spilled south out of Canada and brought an early season snow to eastern Nebraska and west central Iowa.  Even though temperatures dropped into the 20s during the snow, warm ground temperatures caused most of it to melt on roads and most accumulations were limited to grassy surfaces, vehicles, driveways and sidewalks.  However, underneath the heaviest band of snow slushy accumulations were observed even on treated roads.  The snow on paved surfaces quickly melted that morning as temperatures climbed toward and above freezing.  The heaviest snowfall set up along a 30 to 40 mile wide east-west band from Albion and Columbus east through the Omaha metro and into west central Iowa.  Heavier amounts included 7.6 inches in Bennington, 6 inches at Ft. Calhoun, 5.5 inches at the NWS office in Valley and in Schuyler, and 3.5 to 4 inches at Omaha Eppley, near Weston, at Fremont and in Underwood Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An early season storm dropped 2  to 4 inches of snow across much of the county, including around 3 inches in the Gretna area. Due to warm ground temperatures, much of the snow melted on roads as it fell, but the roads did become slushy for a while toward the end of the event until melting completely late that morning or early in the afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low pressure system deepened across the desert southwest, eventually shifting northeast out of the southern Rocky Mountains and across the Central Plains.  The track of this system brought it across south central Nebraska, with the heaviest band of snow associated with the deformation axis clipping far western areas.  The most impact was felt across Dawson County, where snow rates up to 1 to 2 inches per hour allowed for snowfall accumulations ranging from 6 to 13 inches.  Accumuations quickly dropped off east of Cozad, with 3 to 6 inches falling across eastern Dawson and western Gosper and Furnas Counties. Amounts of a trace to 3 inches were reported as far east as a line from Ord to Wilcox.  This heavy snow did cause a brief power outage for approximately 1100 customers near Gothenburg, and lead to a few school closings and postponement of events.

The primary impact from this event was that farmers were once again delayed from harvesting crops in what had already been a late harvest season for many areas.

A potent upper level low pressure system brought heavy snow to much of western and north central Nebraska beginning on October 28th and persisting through October 30th.  Snowfall amounts of 6 to 12 inches fell across the eastern Panhandle on October 28th and 29th. As the upper level low moved from western Kansas into central Nebraska on October 29th and 30th, a swath of very heavy snow fell from southwestern Nebraska into portions of central and north central Nebraska.  Snowfall amounts in these areas ranged from 6 to 15 inches. Gusty north winds of 20 to 35 mph during the early morning hours on October 30th caused blowing and drifting snow with drifts from 3 to 4 feet in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to occasional heavy snowfall occurred across Deuel County beginning during the late afternoon hours on October 29th and persisted through the early morning hours of October 30th.  A cooperative observer 4 miles west of Big Springs reported total snowfall of 10 inches.  Gusty north winds caused blowing and drifting snow with drifts from 3 to 4 feet high.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper level low pressure system over the northern plains combined with an unusually cold air mass that spilled south out of Canada and brought an early season snow to eastern Nebraska and west central Iowa.  Even though temperatures dropped into the 20s during the snow, warm ground temperatures caused most of it to melt on roads and most accumulations were limited to grassy surfaces, vehicles, driveways and sidewalks.  However, underneath the heaviest band of snow slushy accumulations were observed even on treated roads.  The snow on paved surfaces quickly melted that morning as temperatures climbed toward and above freezing.  The heaviest snowfall set up along a 30 to 40 mile wide east-west band from Albion and Columbus east through the Omaha metro and into west central Iowa.  Heavier amounts included 7.6 inches in Bennington, 6 inches at Ft. Calhoun, 5.5 inches at the NWS office in Valley and in Schuyler, and 3.5 to 4 inches at Omaha Eppley, near Weston, at Fremont and in Underwood Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An early season snow dropped 2 to 4 inches across much of the county, including around 4 inches in David City.  Due to warm ground temperatures, much of the snow melted on roads as it fell, but the roads did become slushy for a while toward the end of the event until the snow melted completely later that morning or early afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large and relatively slow moving storm brought a prolonged winter storm and even, for a time, blizzard conditions to eastern Nebraska and western Iowa from late on Dec. 7th through the early morning hours of Dec. 9th.  The storm was driven by an upper level disturbance that tracked from the west coast across the Rockies and deepened over the Mississippi River Valley.  The heaviest snow fell in advance of when the stronger winds arrived, mainly during the morning and afternoon of Dec. 8th.  However, as north winds increased to 30 to 50 mph during the night of the 8th and early on the 9th, visibilities frequently dropped to near zero, especially in open areas.  Considerable drifting snow also occurred, in many cases closing roads back shut almost as fast as they could be opened.  This prompted many counties to pull snow plows off the roads for a while during the night of the 8th and early on the 9th.  Many schools were closed for 3 days because of the storm; due the forecast of heavy snow to begin on the 7th, because of poor visibilities and roads that were drifted shut on the 8th and still some closed roads and bitter cold wind chills which followed the storm on the 9th.  An elderly Omaha man was found dead during the evening of the 8th when he apparently had car trouble and returned to his apartment and was found dead sitting down in a chair outside. ||Total snowfall from the storm was 6 to 15 inches over most of eastern Nebraska and western Iowa.  Higher amounts in eastern Nebraska included 15 inches in Union, 14 inches in Columbus, around 12 inches at the NWS in Valley, at Tekamah, Uehling, Steele City and Weeping Water.  In western Iowa heavier amounts included 12 inches in Clarinda and Hastings and around 11 inches in Harlan, Red Oak and Little Sioux.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 8 to 12 inches fell across much of the county from this storm with around 8 inches measured in Wahoo. The snow began during the late evening of 12/7 and continued into the early morning hours of 12/9.  Winds were not that strong when the snow started, but north winds increased during the afternoon of 12/8 and especially that evening into the next morning.  Frequent wind gusts of 40 mph or more were observed based on observations from surrounding counties. The winds produced considerable blowing and drifting snow with blizzard conditions during the evening and early morning hours of the 8th and 9th. Severe drifting of snow also closed some roads across the county including Highways 77 and 92 near Wahoo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal system moving southeast through the Central Plains became the focus for thunderstorms that produced large hail, heavy rainfall and strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported wind speeds of 60 MPH.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five inch tree branches were broken by thunderstorm wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved southeast and became the focus for severe thunderstorm development into the evening hours across western and north central Nebraska. The storms initiated along the cold front and produced large hail and damaging winds. Heavy rainfall associated with the storms produced localized flash flooding in portions of Holt County.

A thunderstorm complex that developed north of low pressure in north central Kansas merged with thunderstorms that developed north of a warm front that was along the Kansas and Nebraska border.  With temperatures in the 70s and dew point temperatures around 70 near the front and low, instability was moderately high.  Initially the thunderstorms that developed north of the warm front produced a few large hail reports.  However, as the storms to the west merged with the activity farther east the main severe weather reports consisted of strong winds, especially from one cluster of storms that rolled from Saline and Gage county east into the southeast corner of Nebraska.  Around 12,000 customers lost their power from the storm, mostly from Lincoln and locations southwest through southeast of town.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated at 70 mph caused extensive tree damage and some structural damage in the Pleasant Dale area.  The fire station had its roof scattered across the street and couldn't respond to a call due to debris blocking the fire trucks.  Much of the town lost power as well.  The city park sustained damage to a baseball dugout and picnic tables.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed northwest of a stationary front that extended across southeast Nebraska.  The storms were aided by an upper level disturbance that lifted northeast across South Dakota, a modest low level jet and relatively deep low level moisture over eastern Nebraska.  Although the storms dropped some large hail, they mostly produced strong and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to golf ball-size was reported a few miles north of Rogers.  This was from the same cell that produced similar size hail in the Columbus area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed northwest of a stationary front that extended across southeast Nebraska.  The storms were aided by an upper level disturbance that lifted northeast across South Dakota, a modest low level jet and relatively deep low level moisture over eastern Nebraska.  Although the storms dropped some large hail, they mostly produced strong and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: From the same supercell thunderstorm that produced similar size hail in the Columbus area a little earlier.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the morning of August 8, 2009, a cold front pushed southeast through western Nebraska. Deep moisture in advance of the front fueled thunderstorm development into the afternoon. The severe thunderstorms produced large hail and damaging winds in addition to heavy rainfall across southwest Nebraska as storms slowed and trained.

A strong low level jet nosing into the region and good moisture availability resulted in numerous thunderstorms across South Central Nebraska.  Isolated severe thunderstorms developed, with wind gusts estimated at 60 mph reported in Harlan and Buffalo Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated at 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An outflow boundary served as the focus for severe thunderstorms. Thunderstorms developed at peak heating and intensified to produce large hail and damaging winds across portions of west central Nebraska.

The combination of a surface cold front and an upper level disturbance progressing through the region brought late afternoon and evening thunderstorms to portions of South Central Nebraska.  Along with a few reports of penny to nickel size hail across the area, quarter size hail fell near Spalding in Greeley County and in Hubbell in Thayer County.

A weak upper level wave moving across the plains was enough to spark the development of scattered thunderstorms across portions of south central Nebraska.  Modest instability and shear allowed for some thunderstorms to become strong to severe. The most impact was felt across portions of Buffalo, Kearney, and Phelps Counties, where 60 to 80 mph winds and hail to the size of quarters were reported.  Numerous reports of damage came in from the Odessa area, where pivots were overturned, vehicles were blown off of the interstate, and the roof and garage of a home sustained damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level wave moving across the plains was enough to spark the development of scattered thunderstorms across portions of south central Nebraska.  Modest instability and shear allowed for some thunderstorms to become strong to severe. The most impact was felt across portions of Buffalo, Kearney, and Phelps Counties, where 60 to 80 mph winds and hail to the size of quarters were reported.  Numerous reports of damage came in from the Odessa area, where pivots were overturned, vehicles were blown off of the interstate, and the roof and garage of a home sustained damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds estimated at 80 mph caused damage near the town of Odessa.  Reports included center pivots being tipped over, roof and garage damage to a home, widespread tree limbs down, and automobiles being blown off Interstate 80.  Accompanying these winds was hail up to the size of quarters, which combined with the winds caused widespread minor to significant crop damage across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong supercell developed over Hooker County during the late afternoon hours and moved southward.  Strong rotation and large hail was reported with the storm.  Because the storm passed over a very rural area, a tornado could never be confirmed.  Another supercell which produced large hail developed during the evening hours across north central Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported wind gusts of at least 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance crossing the Plains aided in the developement of a MCS, which continued on through the overnight hours as it pushed east into an area of greater instability, while additional elevated thunderstorms developed along the nose of a 30 to 40 knot low level jet.  Some thunderstorms became severe, and though there were a few isolated reports of nickel to quarter size hail, of greater concern was the wind.  There were numerous reports of wind gusts near 60 mph, and one report of a 77 mph wind gust near Benedict in York County.  These winds caused damage to some trees and crops in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific storm brought cold air and abundant moisture to the area. A long duration upslope flow created favorable conditions for heavy snow.  This storm produced dangerous winter storm conditions over a wide area of southeast Wyoming and western Nebraska. Interstates 80 and 25, as well as many state and local highways, were closed for significant periods of time causing problems for travelers and commerce. Law enforcement reported many accidents. There were many reports of snowfall in excess of 20 inches.  As the snowfall was winding down, strong northerly winds were observed creating additional issues with blowing and drifting snow. Reports came in of drifts in the 4 to 6 feet range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter 2 miles south southeast of Harrisburg report 20 to 24 inches of snow with 4 foot drifts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large and relatively slow moving storm brought a prolonged winter storm and even, for a time, blizzard conditions to eastern Nebraska and western Iowa from late on Dec. 7th through the early morning hours of Dec. 9th.  The storm was driven by an upper level disturbance that tracked from the west coast across the Rockies and deepened over the Mississippi River Valley.  The heaviest snow fell in advance of when the stronger winds arrived, mainly during the morning and afternoon of Dec. 8th.  However, as north winds increased to 30 to 50 mph during the night of the 8th and early on the 9th, visibilities frequently dropped to near zero, especially in open areas.  Considerable drifting snow also occurred, in many cases closing roads back shut almost as fast as they could be opened.  This prompted many counties to pull snow plows off the roads for a while during the night of the 8th and early on the 9th.  Many schools were closed for 3 days because of the storm; due the forecast of heavy snow to begin on the 7th, because of poor visibilities and roads that were drifted shut on the 8th and still some closed roads and bitter cold wind chills which followed the storm on the 9th.  An elderly Omaha man was found dead during the evening of the 8th when he apparently had car trouble and returned to his apartment and was found dead sitting down in a chair outside. ||Total snowfall from the storm was 6 to 15 inches over most of eastern Nebraska and western Iowa.  Higher amounts in eastern Nebraska included 15 inches in Union, 14 inches in Columbus, around 12 inches at the NWS in Valley, at Tekamah, Uehling, Steele City and Weeping Water.  In western Iowa heavier amounts included 12 inches in Clarinda and Hastings and around 11 inches in Harlan, Red Oak and Little Sioux.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 8 to 10 inches fell across much of the county with 10 inches reported in Brownville.  The snow began during the evening of 12/7 and continued into the early morning hours of 12/9.  Winds were not that strong when the snow started, but north winds increased during the afternoon of 12/8 and especially that evening into the next morning.  Frequent gusts of over 40 mph were observed based on observations from surrounding counties.  The winds produced considerable blowing and drifting snow which dropped visibilities frequently below 1/4 mile in open areas during the evening and early morning hours of the 8th and 9th.  According to Emergency Management snow plows were unable to make any progress clearing roads and were mainly engaged in pulling stuck emergency vehicles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific storm brought cold air and abundant moisture to the area. A long duration upslope flow created favorable conditions for heavy snow.  This storm produced dangerous winter storm conditions over a wide area of southeast Wyoming and western Nebraska. Interstates 80 and 25, as well as many state and local highways, were closed for significant periods of time causing problems for travelers and commerce. Law enforcement reported many accidents. There were many reports of snowfall in excess of 20 inches.  As the snowfall was winding down, strong northerly winds were observed creating additional issues with blowing and drifting snow. Reports came in of drifts in the 4 to 6 feet range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cooperative observer in Hemingford reported 6 inches of snow.

An upper level disturbance working its way across the Plains worked in conjunction with a slow moving surface frontal boundary to produce thunderstorms across South Central Nebraska.  With plenty of moisture available, these thunderstorms brought heavy rain to a good portion of the area, with Adams and Clay Counties the hardest hit.  While anywhere from 2 to 4 inches were reported in portions of Hamilton, York, and Fillmore Counties, 3 to 5 inches fell in northern Adams and Clay Counties. The underpass in Hastings filled with water, with one motorists becoming stranded and needed rescue, and travelling on area county roads was difficult. Along with the numerous reports of county roads under water, portions of Highway 281 in northern Adams County and Highway 6 in Fillmore County were covered by a few inches of water.  The impact of this heavy rain was felt well into the 27th, before water levels finally receded.  Any reports of damage were to country roads and culverts.

An isolated severe thunderstorm moving across Fillmore Country dropped guarter size hail near the town of Shickley.

An upper level disturbance crossing the Plains aided in the developement of a MCS, which continued on through the overnight hours as it pushed east into an area of greater instability, while additional elevated thunderstorms developed along the nose of a 30 to 40 knot low level jet.  Some thunderstorms became severe, and though there were a few isolated reports of nickel to quarter size hail, of greater concern was the wind.  There were numerous reports of wind gusts near 60 mph, and one report of a 77 mph wind gust near Benedict in York County.  These winds caused damage to some trees and crops in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large trees in town were blown down.  Some crop damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving winter storm produced one to six inches of snow across extreme southwest Nebraska.  Eighteen utility poles were broken from the weight of heavy, wet snow, resulting in power outages in parts of Dundy and Hitchcock counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observers reported snowfall totals of 1 inch in the southeast part of the county to near 6 inches in the northwest. The heavy, wet snow broke tree limbs and utility poles resulting in power outages in the area, mainly in northwest Hitchcock county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level disturbance moving through the region sparked another round of thunderstorms across the Plains.  Aided by the nose of a 40 to 50 knot low level jet, early morning thunderstorms developed across portions of South Central Nebraska.  Some thunderstorms became severe, moving across areas north of Interstate 80.  Hail up to the size of golf balls was reported, causing some crop damage across Nance County.
An isolated severe thunderstorm moved across Valley County, dropping quarter size hail near Ord.

The combination of a surface cold front and an upper level disturbance progressing through the region brought late afternoon and evening thunderstorms to portions of South Central Nebraska.  Along with a few reports of penny to nickel size hail across the area, quarter size hail fell near Spalding in Greeley County and in Hubbell in Thayer County.
And isolated severe thunderstorm pushed through Valley County during the late evening hours, dropping quarter to ping pong ball size hail and producing 60 mph wind gusts near Ord.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter to ping pong ball size hail was reported, was accompanied by wind gusts of 60 mph.

An upper level disturbance crossing the Plains aided in the developement of a MCS, which continued on through the overnight hours as it pushed east into an area of greater instability, while additional elevated thunderstorms developed along the nose of a 30 to 40 knot low level jet.  Some thunderstorms became severe, and though there were a few isolated reports of nickel to quarter size hail, of greater concern was the wind.  There were numerous reports of wind gusts near 60 mph, and one report of a 77 mph wind gust near Benedict in York County.  These winds caused damage to some trees and crops in the area.

A strong Pacific storm brought cold air and abundant moisture to the area. A long duration upslope flow created favorable conditions for heavy snow.  This storm produced dangerous winter storm conditions over a wide area of southeast Wyoming and western Nebraska. Interstates 80 and 25, as well as many state and local highways, were closed for significant periods of time causing problems for travelers and commerce. Law enforcement reported many accidents. There were many reports of snowfall in excess of 20 inches.  As the snowfall was winding down, strong northerly winds were observed creating additional issues with blowing and drifting snow. Reports came in of drifts in the 4 to 6 feet range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter 11 miles southeast of Kimball reported 14 inches of snow at 8 am.  Blowing snow also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large and relatively slow moving storm brought a prolonged winter storm and even, for a time, blizzard conditions to eastern Nebraska and western Iowa from late on Dec. 7th through the early morning hours of Dec. 9th.  The storm was driven by an upper level disturbance that tracked from the west coast across the Rockies and deepened over the Mississippi River Valley.  The heaviest snow fell in advance of when the stronger winds arrived, mainly during the morning and afternoon of Dec. 8th.  However, as north winds increased to 30 to 50 mph during the night of the 8th and early on the 9th, visibilities frequently dropped to near zero, especially in open areas.  Considerable drifting snow also occurred, in many cases closing roads back shut almost as fast as they could be opened.  This prompted many counties to pull snow plows off the roads for a while during the night of the 8th and early on the 9th.  Many schools were closed for 3 days because of the storm; due the forecast of heavy snow to begin on the 7th, because of poor visibilities and roads that were drifted shut on the 8th and still some closed roads and bitter cold wind chills which followed the storm on the 9th.  An elderly Omaha man was found dead during the evening of the 8th when he apparently had car trouble and returned to his apartment and was found dead sitting down in a chair outside. ||Total snowfall from the storm was 6 to 15 inches over most of eastern Nebraska and western Iowa.  Higher amounts in eastern Nebraska included 15 inches in Union, 14 inches in Columbus, around 12 inches at the NWS in Valley, at Tekamah, Uehling, Steele City and Weeping Water.  In western Iowa heavier amounts included 12 inches in Clarinda and Hastings and around 11 inches in Harlan, Red Oak and Little Sioux.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 8 to 15 inches fell across much of the county from this storm with 15 inches measured in Union and 12 inches reported at Weeping Water. The snow began during the evening of 12/7 and continued into the early morning hours of 12/9.  Winds were not that strong when the snow started, but north winds increased during the afternoon of 12/8 and especially that evening into the next morning.  Frequent wind gusts of 40 mph or more were observed with a peak gust of 45 mph measured at the Plattsmouth AWOS.  The winds produced considerable blowing and drifting snow with blizzard conditions during the evening and early morning hours of the 8th and 9th. Emergency Management reported that road crews were unable to keep roads clear because of the severe drifting.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure area moved from the Central Rockies across the Central High Plains, producing an area of heavy snow over portions of northwest Kansas and southwest Nebraska.  Snowfall up to 11 inches was reported.  Gusty north winds resulted in some blowing and drifting of snow.  Maximum snow totals in Nebraska included: Six inches in Hitchcock and Red Willow counties.  In Kansas, six inches fell in Rawlins, Sherman and Wallace counties, seven inches in Thomas and Logan counties, eight inches in Sheridan county, ten inches in Decatur, Norton and Gove counties and eleven inches in Graham county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to seven inches of snow was reported in Red Willow County, with 1-foot drifts reported near Indianola.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms developed during the evening hours as an upper level disturbance pushed a surface cold front across south central Nebraska.  With ample instability and shear for these thunderstorms to work with, hail up to the size of ping pong balls and winds of 60 mph were reported.

Scattered thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours across northern and eastern portions of South Central Nebraska along weak surface boundaries left over from previous convection.  Some thunderstorms became strong to severe, dropping nickel to quarter size hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime to nickel size hail was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the early hours on the morning, a lightning strike ignited a fire to hay bales.  Forty-eight large round hay bales were totally lost.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck and burned 48 large round hay bales.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several clusters of severe thunderstorms developed over southwest Nebraska and northwest Kansas during the late afternoon and evening hours.  Numerous reports of large hail were received, and two brief tornadoes were witnessed by storm chasers in Gove county Kansas.

A weak upper level wave moving across the plains was enough to spark the development of scattered thunderstorms across portions of south central Nebraska.  Modest instability and shear allowed for some thunderstorms to become strong to severe. The most impact was felt across portions of Buffalo, Kearney, and Phelps Counties, where 60 to 80 mph winds and hail to the size of quarters were reported.  Numerous reports of damage came in from the Odessa area, where pivots were overturned, vehicles were blown off of the interstate, and the roof and garage of a home sustained damage.
A cold front sweeping southeast across the area help produce thunderstorms during the late afternoon and early evening hours.  Large hail and gusty winds were associated with the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The strong winds rattled windows.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the morning of August 8, 2009, a cold front pushed southeast through western Nebraska. Deep moisture in advance of the front fueled thunderstorm development into the afternoon. The severe thunderstorms produced large hail and damaging winds in addition to heavy rainfall across southwest Nebraska as storms slowed and trained.

Numerous thunderstorms moved northeast out of Colorado and into southwest Nebraska during the afternoon hours. The storms initially produced large hail.  However, then the storms started to develop into a bow echo as they moved northeast and produced very strong winds and a couple of brief tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The winds broke off large tree limbs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced some hail around Bayard, Nebraska.
Thundertorms, some severe, produced flash flooding and a few tornadoes over parts of the southern Nebraska panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brief tornado reported over open country.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thundertorms, some severe, produced flash flooding and a few tornadoes over parts of the southern Nebraska panhandle.

A strong supercell developed over Hooker County during the late afternoon hours and moved southward.  Strong rotation and large hail was reported with the storm.  Because the storm passed over a very rural area, a tornado could never be confirmed.  Another supercell which produced large hail developed during the evening hours across north central Nebraska.
Scattered multicell thunderstorms produced hail and strong winds across southwest Nebraska during the afternoon and evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail stripped leaves off of trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered multicell thunderstorms produced hail and strong winds across southwest Nebraska during the afternoon and evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large hail accompanied the strong thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of storms produced hail and strong winds over parts of the western Nebraska panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sudden wind gust blew over a tractor-trailer on Interstate 80 near Kimball, Ne.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low level jet combined with abundant moisture to produce overnight thunderstorms that lasted until after daybreak.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The strong thunderstorm winds snapped a traffic light pole and broke off 10 to 15 inch diameter tree limbs.  Some of the tree limbs fell on homes and power lines.  A large part of Broken Bow was without power for a period of time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front which moved into southeast Nebraska during the morning of June 6th stalled from southeast Nebraska into central Kansas during the afternoon and evening of that day.  Temperatures by late afternoon climbed into the upper 80s and lower 90s south of the front while readings northwest of the front were in the 50s and 60s. Thunderstorms developed near the boundary over Kansas early in the evening, and developed or spread into southeast Nebraska during the mid evening hours.  The storms produced mainly large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter-size hail was reported from around 1 mile southwest of Western to around 1 mile east of town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced large hail in Dixon County of extreme northeast Nebraska on the early morning of may 5th.
An isolated multicell thunderstorm produced hail in north central Nebraska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain also fell with the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front kicked off a few severe thunderstorms over eastern Nebraska during the mid evening hours of July 24th.  Initially warm mid level temperatures had hindered development, but they were overcome as the front encountered a bit better low level inflow and instability.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few hailstones up to nickel-size fell for a minute or two in the Wagner Lakes region in southwest Columbus.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms moved northeast out of Colorado and into southwest Nebraska during the afternoon hours. The storms initially produced large hail.  However, then the storms started to develop into a bow echo as they moved northeast and produced very strong winds and a couple of brief tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cooperative observer reported wind gusts in excess of 60 mph along with brief heavy rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms over the Nebraska Panhandle produced hail and strong winds as they moved southeast and weakened during the evening hours.

Supercells developed rapidly in the late afternoon and produced numerous funnels and large hail. A nearly stationary supercell near Sutherland produced one tornado which a storm spotter caught on video. The slow movement of the storms also produced torrential rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS employee reported a funnel cloud.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe storm produced some hail and strong winds over parts of Dawes county.

Hot and unstable air, characterized by temperatures in the 90s and dew point temperatures in the 70s, stretched from eastern Kansas into south central Nebraska by mid afternoon on June 17th.  Northeast of that region, over most of eastern Nebraska, temperatures were also quite warm, in the upper 80s to lower 90s, but dew point temperatures were a bit lower, mostly in the 60s.  Supercell thunderstorms developed along this moisture discontinuity near the Kansas border during the early afternoon hours.  At least one of these storms produced a tornado.  Later in the afternoon and into the evening other storms that developed in central Nebraska on the nose of the warm and unstable air moved across the region producing mainly large hail but also areas of damaging winds.
An isolated storm produced some large hail over far northwestern Kimball county.

A stalled front extending from extreme southeast Nebraska into central Kansas, and an upper level low pressure trough to the west, brought scattered severe thunderstorms to eastern Nebraska during much of Sunday June 7th into the very early morning hours of June 8th.  Temperatures climbed into the lower 80s in far southeast Nebraska and southwest Iowa on the 7th, while readings remained in the 50s in northeast Nebraska.  Elevated storms, a few of which dropped large hail, moved across northeast Nebraska from late morning through the afternoon hours of the 7th.  By late afternoon of that day several supercell thunderstorms developed along the stalled front.  These storms shifted out of the region by early evening but additional thunderstorms that developed near the boundary in Kansas moved back into southeast Nebraska later that evening into the early overnight hours.  Although the environment was favorable for tornadoes this evening, in Nebraska the storms produced large hail and a few strong wind reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to half dollar-size was accompanied by thunderstorm winds that also downed a few trees.  The wind driven hail damaged numerous homes and cars in and around Adams and also caused crop damage in the area.  One local insurance agent had already received 50 agriculture/crop claims and 150 house and auto claims by Monday morning June 8th, with many, many, many claims against roof, siding and paint damage to come he stated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed and moved east during the early morning hours.

An outflow boundary served as the focus for severe thunderstorms. Thunderstorms developed at peak heating and intensified to produce large hail and damaging winds across portions of west central Nebraska.

A thunderstorm complex that developed north of low pressure in north central Kansas merged with thunderstorms that developed north of a warm front that was along the Kansas and Nebraska border.  With temperatures in the 70s and dew point temperatures around 70 near the front and low, instability was moderately high.  Initially the thunderstorms that developed north of the warm front produced a few large hail reports.  However, as the storms to the west merged with the activity farther east the main severe weather reports consisted of strong winds, especially from one cluster of storms that rolled from Saline and Gage county east into the southeast corner of Nebraska.  Around 12,000 customers lost their power from the storm, mostly from Lincoln and locations southwest through southeast of town.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at around 70 mph blew the tin off of outbuildings and knocked down large tree limbs near Tecumseh which caused some home damage.  Around 200 residents lost power also.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thundertorms, some severe, produced flash flooding and a few tornadoes over parts of the southern Nebraska panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall washed out some county roads around Scottsbluuf as well as flooded some buildings at the campus of Western Nebraska Community College.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level disturbance moving through the region sparked another round of thunderstorms across the Plains.  Aided by the nose of a 40 to 50 knot low level jet, early morning thunderstorms developed across portions of South Central Nebraska.  Some thunderstorms became severe, moving across areas north of Interstate 80.  Hail up to the size of golf balls was reported, causing some crop damage across Nance County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail accompanied by wind gusts estimated at 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a surface cold front and an upper level disturbance progressing through the region brought late afternoon and evening thunderstorms to portions of South Central Nebraska.  Along with a few reports of penny to nickel size hail across the area, quarter size hail fell near Spalding in Greeley County and in Hubbell in Thayer County.

Numerous thunderstorms moved northeast out of Colorado and into southwest Nebraska during the afternoon hours. The storms initially produced large hail.  However, then the storms started to develop into a bow echo as they moved northeast and produced very strong winds and a couple of brief tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The very strong winds snapped off large trees, some of which fell on roofs of homes and on cars, and caused roof damage at the lumber yard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front which moved into southeast Nebraska during the morning of June 6th stalled from southeast Nebraska into central Kansas during the afternoon and evening of that day.  Temperatures by late afternoon climbed into the upper 80s and lower 90s south of the front while readings northwest of the front were in the 50s and 60s. Thunderstorms developed near the boundary over Kansas early in the evening, and developed or spread into southeast Nebraska during the mid evening hours.  The storms produced mainly large hail.
Storms initially developed across north central Nebraska and then spread slowly southeast.  The storms contained large hail and heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported 1.5 inches of rain along with the hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered multicell thunderstorms produced hail and strong winds across southwest Nebraska during the afternoon and evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The strong thunderstorm winds were accompanied by hail ranging from pea to dime size.  The storm severely damaged crops.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms that developed west of the region moved into eastern Nebraska and western Iowa during the early morning hours of June 19th.  A fairly strong cap was overcome, aided by a low level jet and an upper level disturbance which provided some cooling aloft.  The strongest storms occurred in Butler county Nebraska as a cluster bowed out producing scattered wind damage.  Although the thunderstorms weakened somewhat, the storms continued to produce occasional wind damage as they progressed into western Iowa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated at over 70 mph knocked a very large tree onto a garage destroying half of it.  Other tree damage was noted in town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening of June 18th and early morning hours of June 19th, supercell storm developed over western Nebraska and produced strong winds and large hail.
An area of thunderstorms produced strong outflow winds over much of the southern and central portions of the Nebraska panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gust recorded at Scotts Bluff National Monument.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of thunderstorms produced strong outflow winds over much of the southern and central portions of the Nebraska panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds damaged some trees.
